2.
DAVIS-BACON ACT
For all prime construction, alteration or repair contracts in excess of $2,000 awarded by FTA, the 
Contractor shall comply with the Davis-Bacon Act and the Copeland “Anti-Kickback” Act. Under 49 
U.S.C. § 5333(a), prevailing wage protections apply to laborers and mechanics employed on FTA 
assisted construction, alteration, or repair projects. The Contractor will comply with the Davis-Bacon 
Act, 40 U.S.C. §§ 3141-3144, and 3146-3148 as supplemented by DOL regulations at 29 C.F.R. part 5, 
“Labor Standards Provisions Applicable to Contracts Governing Federally Financed and Assisted 
Construction.” In accordance with the statute, the Contractor shall pay wages to laborers and mechanics 
at a rate not less than the prevailing wages specified in a wage determination made by the Secretary of 
Labor. In addition, the Contractor agrees to pay wages not less than once a week. The Contractor shall 
also comply with the Copeland “Anti-Kickback” Act (40 U.S.C. § 3145), as supplemented by DOL 
regulations at 29 C.F.R. part 3, “Contractors and Subcontractors on Public Building or Public Work 
Financed in Whole or in part by Loans or Grants from the United States.” The Contractor is prohibited 
from inducing, by any means, any person employed in the construction, completion, or repair of public 
work, to give up any part of the compensation to which he or she is otherwise entitled.
3.
CONTRACT WORK HOURS AND SAFETY STANDARDS ACT 
For all contracts in excess of $100,000 that involve the employment of mechanics or laborers, the 
Contractor shall comply with the Contract Work Hours and Safety Standards Act (40 U.S.C. §§ 3701; 
3708), as supplemented by the DOL regulations at 29 C.F.R. part 5.  Under 40 U.S.C. § 3702 of the Act, 
the Contractor shall compute the wages of every mechanic and laborer, including watchmen and guards, 
on the basis of a standard work week of 40 hours.  Work in excess of the standard work week is 
permissible provided that the worker is compensated at a rate of not less than one and a half times the 
basic rate of pay for all hours worked in excess of 40 hours in the work week.  The requirements of 40 
U.S.C. § 3704 are applicable to construction work and provide that no laborer or mechanic be required 
to work in surroundings or under working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous or dangerous.  
These requirements do not apply to the purchase of supplies or materials or articles ordinarily available 
on the open market, or to contracts for transportation or transmission of intelligence.
In the event of any violation of the clause set forth herein, the Contractor and any subcontractor 
responsible therefor shall be liable for the unpaid wages. In addition, the Contractor and subcontractor 
shall be liable to the United States (in the case of work done under contract for the District of Columbia 
or a territory, to such District or to such territory), for liquidated damages. Such liquidated damages 
shall be computed with respect to each individual laborer or mechanic, including watchmen and guards, 
employed in violation of this clause in the sum of $10 for each calendar day on which such individual 
was required or permitted to work in excess of the standard workweek of forty hours without payment of 
the overtime wages required by this clause.
The FTA shall upon its own action or upon written request of an authorized representative of the 
Department of Labor withhold or cause to be withheld, from any moneys payable on account of work 
performed by the Contractor or subcontractor under any such contract or any other Federal contract with 
the same prime Contractor, or any other federally-assisted contract subject to the Contract Work Hours 
and Safety Standards Act, which is held by the same prime Contractor, such sums as may be determined 
to be necessary to satisfy any liabilities of such Contractor or subcontractor for unpaid wages and 
liquidated damages as provided in this section.
The Contractor or subcontractor shall insert in any subcontracts the clauses set forth in this section and 
also a clause requiring the subcontractors to include these clauses in any lower tier subcontracts. The 
146 of 570

--- page 147 ---
MTC/City of San Mateo
Master Funding Agreement
Page 39
prime Contractor shall be responsible for compliance by any subcontractor or lower tier subcontractor 
with the clauses set forth in this agreement. 
4.
COPELAND ANTI-KICKBACK ACT 
Compliance with Copeland Act requirements - The AGENCY’s contractor shall comply with the 
requirements of 29 CFR part 3, which are incorporated by reference in this contract.
5.
PROMPT PAYMENT OF FUNDS WITHHELD TO SUBCONTRACTORS 
The AGENCY shall hold retainage from the prime contractor and shall make prompt and regular 
incremental acceptances of portions, as determined by the agency of the contract work and pay retainage 
to the prime contractor based on these acceptances.  The AGENCY’s prime contractor or subcontractor 
shall return all monies withheld in retention from a subcontractor within 30 days after receiving payment 
for work satisfactorily completed and accepted including incremental acceptances of portions of the 
contract work by the agency.  Federal regulation (49 CFR 26.29) requires that any delay or 
postponement of payment over 30 days may take place only for good cause and with the agency’s prior 
written approval.  Any violation of this provision shall subject the violating AGENCY’s prime 
contractor or subcontractor to the penalties, sanctions, and other remedies specified in Section 7108.5 of 
the California Business and Professions Code.  These requirements shall not be construed to limit or 
impair any contractual, administrative, or judicial remedies otherwise, available to the prime contractor 
or subcontractor in the event of a dispute involving late payment, or nonpayment by the prime 
contractor, deficient subcontract performance, or noncompliance by a subcontractor.  This provision 
applies to both DBE and non-DBE prime contractors and subcontractors.

EXHIBIT B-6
ADDITIONAL TERMS AND CONDITIONS 
(REGIONAL TOLL FUNDS INCLUDING RM1, RM2, RM3, AND AB 1171)
Recitals
WHEREAS, Streets and Highways Code (SHC) Sections 30950 et seq. created the Bay Area Toll 
Authority (“BATA”) which is a public instrumentality governed by the same board as that governing 
MTC; and
WHEREAS, pursuant to SHC Section 31010(b), funds generated in excess of those needed to meet 
the toll commitments as specified by paragraph (4) of subdivision (b) of Section 188.5 of the SHC shall 
be available to BATA for funding projects consistent with SHC Sections 30913 and 30914; and
WHEREAS, MTC adopted Resolution No. 3434, Revised, which establishes commitments of 
bridge toll funds, including such AB 1171 funds, to specific projects and corridors; and
WHEREAS, on November 8, 1988, voters approved Regional Measure 1 (“RM1”), which 
authorized a standard auto toll of $1 on the seven state-owned toll bridges in the San Francisco Bay Area 
to fund various transportation projects within the region; and
WHEREAS, on March 2, 2004, voters approved Regional Measure 2 (“RM2”), increasing the toll 
for all vehicles on the seven state-owned toll bridges in the San Francisco Bay Area by $1.00 to fund 
various transportation projects within the region that have been determined to reduce congestion or to 
improve travel in the toll bridge corridors; and
WHEREAS, RM2 established the Regional Traffic Relief Plan and listed specific capital projects 
and programs and transit operating assistance as eligible to receive RM2 funding as identified in SHC 
Section 30914(c) and (d). The funding amounts assigned to certain of the programs and projects were 
subsequently revised by MTC Resolution No. 3801; and
WHEREAS, to the extent the Project is receiving RM2 funding hereunder, SHC Section 30914(c) 
lists the Project to which this Exhibit B-6 and the applicable Supplement apply as one such eligible 
transportation project and designates AGENCY as project sponsor; and
WHEREAS, pursuant to MTC Resolution No. 3636, MTC established procedures whereby 
eligible transportation project sponsors may submit allocation requests for Regional Measure 2 Bridge 
Toll funding.  A copy of MTC Resolution No. 3636 is attached hereto and incorporated herein as 
Attachment D, MTC Resolution No. 3636; and
WHEREAS, on June 5, 2018, voters approved Regional Measure 3 (“RM3”), increasing the toll 
for all vehicles on the seven state-owned toll bridges in the San Francisco Bay Area by three successive 
$1.00 tolls (applied on January 1, 2019, January 1, 2022, and January 1, 2025) to fund various 
transportation projects within the region that have been determined to reduce congestion or to improve 
travel in the toll bridge corridors; and
148 of 570

--- page 149 ---
MTC/City of San Mateo
Master Funding Agreement
Page 41
WHEREAS, RM3 established the Regional Measure 3 Expenditure Plan and listed specific capital 
projects and operating assistance programs as eligible to receive RM3 funding as identified in SHC 
Section 30914.7(a) and (c); and
WHEREAS, to the extent the Project is receiving RM3 funding hereunder, SHC Section 
30914.7(a) lists the Project or SHC Section 30914.7(c) lists the operating assistance program to which this 
Exhibit B-6 and the applicable Supplement apply as one such eligible transportation project and designates 
AGENCY as project sponsor; and
WHEREAS, pursuant to MTC Resolution No. 4404, MTC established procedures whereby 
eligible transportation project sponsors may submit allocation requests for RM3 Bridge Toll funding.  A 
copy of MTC Resolution No. 4404 is attached hereto and incorporated herein as Attachment E, MTC 
Resolution No. 4404; and
WHEREAS, AGENCY submitted one or more allocation requests for RM2, RM3, AB 1171, 
and/or RM1 funding for the Project to which this Exhibit B-6 applies.  A copy of the applicable allocation 
request(s) as well as AGENCY’s resolution(s) approving the allocation request(s) are attached to the 
applicable Supplement and incorporated herein as Attachment A, Updated Initial Project Report, and 
Attachment B, AGENCY Resolution(s), respectively; and
WHEREAS, by the resolution(s) attached to the applicable Supplement and incorporated herein 
as Attachment C, MTC Resolution(s) Approving Project Request, MTC approved AGENCY’s request(s) 
for the applicable funds for the applicable Project.
I.
AGENCY AGREES
A.
AGENCY agrees to perform or caused to be performed the activities described in Attachment A, 
Updated Initial Project Report. AGENCY will provide all necessary staffing and support resources to 
complete the Project as described in Attachment A, AGENCY agrees to meet all conditions listed in 
Attachment C, MTC Resolution(s) Approving Project Request.
B.
AGENCY shall provide MTC with annual progress reports throughout the term of this Agreement 
in accordance with the monitoring and reporting requirements specified in MTC Resolution No. 3636, 
Revised (if receiving RM2 funds) and/or MTC Resolution No. 4404 (if receiving RM3 funds). 
C.
AGENCY shall submit invoices to MTC no less than annually, but may submit invoices as 
frequently as monthly.  In either case, AGENCY shall submit an invoice to MTC within thirty (30) days 
after the end of each period for which payment is sought covering costs for the Project activities 
accomplished through the end of such period, not covered by previously submitted invoices.  Each invoice 
shall be supported by the following information:  (i.) A brief narrative progress report of the activities 
accomplished during the invoice period, including the percentage of the contract complete and the 
percentage of funding expended; (ii.) the costs requested for reimbursement with RM1, RM2, RM3 and/or 
AB 1171 funds, as applicable; (iii.) the total costs expended for  the invoice period broken down by type 
and source of funding; (iv.) the total RM1, RM2, RM3 and/or AB 1171 funds, as applicable received as

which AGENCY is in default.  If AGENCY does not cure the breach or describe to MTC’s satisfaction a 
plan for curing the breach within the sixty (60) day period, MTC may terminate this Agreement or the 
applicable Supplement for cause.  In the event of such termination for cause, AGENCY will be entitled 
only to those costs incurred for already completed Project work, not to exceed the maximum amount

1.
TERMINATION
Notwithstanding Article 8.0, TERMINATION in the Master Funding Agreement, MTC may 
terminate this Agreement without cause upon ten (10) days prior written notice.  If MTC terminates this 
Agreement without cause, AGENCY shall be entitled to payment for costs incurred for incomplete 
deliverables, up to the maximum amount payable for each deliverable.  If AGENCY fails to perform as 
specified in this Agreement, MTC may terminate this Agreement for cause by written notice and 
AGENCY shall be entitled only to costs incurred for work product acceptable to MTC, not to exceed the 
maximum amount payable under this Agreement for such work product.
2.
RETENTION OF RECORDS
AGENCY agrees to establish and maintain an accounting system confirming to GAAP that is 
adequate to accumulate and segregate reasonable, allowable, and allocable project costs.
AGENCY further agrees to keep all records pertaining to the project being funded for audit 
purposes for a minimum of three (3) years following final payment to AGENCY or four (4) years 
following the fiscal year of the last expenditure under this Agreement, whichever is longer, in 
accordance with generally accepted accounting principles.  Copies of AGENCY audits, if any, 
performed during the course of Project development and at Project completion shall be forwarded to 
MTC no later than one hundred eighty (180) days after fiscal year end close. 
3.
AUDITS
Notwithstanding Article 13.0, AUDITS in the Master Funding Agreement, AGENCY agrees to 
grant MTC, or any agency that provides MTC with funds for the Project, including but not limited to, 
the U.S. Department of Transportation, FHWA, the Comptroller General of the United States, the State, 
and their authorized representatives access to AGENCY’s books and records for the purpose of 
verifying that funds are properly accounted for and proceeds are expended in accordance with the terms 
of this Agreement.  All documents shall be available for inspection during normal business hours at any 
time while the Project is underway and for the retention period specified in Article 4.
AGENCY further agrees to include in all its third-party contracts hereunder a provision to the 
effect that the contractor agrees that MTC, the U.S. Department of Transportation, FHWA, the 
Comptroller General of the United States, the State, or any of their duly authorized representatives shall 
have access to and the right to examine any directly pertinent books, documents, papers, and records of 
such subcontractor, during normal business hours, for the term specified above.  The term “contract” as 
used in this clause excludes agreements not exceeding $25,000.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall

Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR

City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
March 17, 2025 
SUBJECT:
4th and Railroad Parking Lot, Bespoke Development – Exclusive Negotiation Agreement Extension
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve Amendment No. 2 to the agreement with DTSM Housing LLC for exclusive negotiations 
related to the public-private partnership development project, commonly known as Bespoke, located partially on City 
owned property at 4th and Railroad, for a one-time extension of six (6) months from April 14, 2025, to October 14, 2025, 
approve the proposed schedule of performance, and authorize the City Manager to execute this amendment.
BACKGROUND:
The City is currently a party to an Exclusive Negotiating Agreement (ENA) for the redevelopment of the City-owned surface
parking lot located at 4th and Railroad Avenue adjacent to the parcel where the former Talbot’s building is located. The
ENA is with a three-party development team comprised of Prometheus, Harvest Properties, and Alta Housing (a non-profit
affordable housing developer) and is intended to be in place throughout the entitlement phase of the project. The City
entered into the ENA in October 2021 (Attachment 2) and has since been working with the development team to refine
the project, now referred to as Bespoke, through the planning entitlement process, and prepare legal agreements that 
would accompany the entitlements. The ENA was amended in October 2024 to extend the term to April 14, 2025 
(Attachment 3) and the proposed amendment would extend the ENA to October 14, 2025 (Attachment 4).
The Project includes a parcel owned by the City, which means the City is not merely acting in a regulatory capacity but is
also acting in a proprietary capacity as a partner. As the project has evolved, so have the proposed deal terms related to
the City’s provision of land and funding support for the affordable housing component of the project. Deal terms
were last considered by Council at a study session on September 16, 2024 (Agenda Report included as Attachment 5). The 
critical items considered by Council at that meeting included assurances from the commercial development team should 
the affordable housing project on City land fail to secure funding and begin construction, and the project schedule for 
completion. The ENA was also set to expire on October 14, 2024, but consistent with City Council direction at this meeting, 
it was extended for six months to April 14, 2025, pursuant to ENA Section 1.3(b)(1).
This report is intended to provide the City Council with an update on the project status and to seek an additional six-month 
extension to allow for completion of all legal agreements and timely scheduling of public hearings before the Planning 
Commission and City Council for project approval. 
Separate from the legal agreements, the Bespoke project completed the pre-application phase of its planning entitlement 
review and submitted their formal planning application in February 2023. The project was deemed complete on July 17, 
2024, and the environmental analysis, in the form of an Initial Study/Mitigated Negative Declaration, was circulated for 
public review and comment between October 4, 2024 and November 4, 2024. Ten comments were received and the City is 
preparing final responses to comments, in preparation for the project’s public hearings, which are anticipated later this 
157 of 570

--- page 158 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
year (spring/summer). 
Project History
The City Council adopted a Resolution declaring the surface parking lot at East 4th Avenue and South Railroad Avenue, 
known as the Talbot’s parking lot, as “Surplus Land” at their meeting on January 4, 2021, and, on July 19, 2021, the City 
Council directed staff to negotiate an Exclusive Negotiating Agreement (ENA) with the Prometheus/Harvest 
Properties/Alta Housing team (Development Team) to explore the redevelopment of the City’s parking lot site for a mixed-
use development project. The City Council subsequently approved the ENA at their meeting on October 4, 2021. 
The Development Team was selected based on their proposal to develop the full block; remove awkward transition and 
existing easement issues; and provide a mix of unit sizes that make the project family friendly, the highest ratio of on-site 
parking for the housing; and the ability to serve income levels of up to 70 percent of Area Median Income (AMI), which 
addressed the Council’s interest in serving both low- and middle-income households. A mixed-use project on this block 
was also identified as being able to help re-energize this part of B Street, have synergy with the project at the adjacent 
Draeger’s Market site, and contribute to the overall economic vitality of downtown.
The proposed Project includes the following components:  
•
A seven-story, 71-unit 100 percent affordable housing building on the City’s lot with community space for Self-
Help for the Elderly on the first floor;   
•
A six-story 156,000 square-foot commercial and office building;   
•
137 parking spaces on-site in an underground parking garage (of which 37 would be for residents' use and 25 
would be shared between commercial and residential uses); access would be on City lot; the remaining 
commercial garage would be accessible to the public on evenings and weekends;
•
A combination of at-grade open space and roof top common areas; and   
•
Improvements in the public right-of-way area along all four street frontages.   
Key Deal Terms
In May 2023 and September 2024, additional draft deal terms were presented to Council that form the basis of the 
Development Agreement (DA)/Disposition and Development Loan Agreement (DDLA) that the City and Development Team 
would enter into at the time that the project receives its entitlements. After receiving direction from the City Council at 
the September 2024 study session, the City and Development Team have continued to refine these terms while developing 
the DA/DDLA. A summary of the key project terms is provided below. It is important to note that each of these terms will 
be memorialized in greater detail in the applicable legal agreements:
•
Alta Housing (Affordable Housing Developer) will enter into a 99-year ground lease with the City at the rate of $1 
per year, and the Development Team will enter into a reciprocal easement agreement for the City’s parcel. 
•
The City will contribute $3 million (m) to support the construction of the affordable housing from the City’s 
dedicated affordable housing funds. The specific source of funds from within the City’s housing funds is still to be 
determined. 
•
Prometheus/Harvest Properties (Commercial Developer) will contribute $2.9 million (m) to support the affordable 
housing project, which will be used for pre-development costs, as defined in the DDLA, such as architectural and 
engineering plans, upon City approval. The City will disburse the $2.9m to the Residential Developer in the form of 
a zero-interest, forgivable predevelopment loan. 
•
The Commercial Developer will finance and construct the shared underground parking garage across the entire 
site, including mechanical lift spaces dedicated to affordable housing, and create separate pads for the 
development of the affordable housing building and the commercial building, as described in the September 16, 
2024 City Council study session.
 
•
The Affordable Housing Developer will provide ground floor space in the affordable housing building at a 
158 of 570

--- page 159 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
substantially subsidized rent (the proposal is $1 per year) to Self-Help for the Elderly to allow for their relocation 
from the Central Park Recreation Center, which will be necessitated by the implementation of the Central Park 
Master Plan.
•
In order to allow for the construction of the underground parking garage across parcel lines, the City and the 
Development Team will execute a lot-tie agreement.
•
The Commercial Developer will provide additional assurances, including financial assurances in the form of 
deposits, given the project phasing. Due to the construction readiness requirements of affordable housing 
financing, the underground parking garage must commence construction and be substantially completed prior to 
the affordable housing project applying for federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credits (tax credits). The project 
application for tax credits also must be closely coordinated with other state and local funding sources, which must 
be obtained first. As a result, the commercial component is highly likely to be completed prior to the housing 
component. The length of this differential between the completion of the commercial and the affordable housing 
components of the project will also depend on whether the affordable housing goes through multiple cycles of 
funding requests in order to assemble its financing. If the residential meets the relevant milestones in the Schedule 
of Performance, (Attachment 6), these monies would no longer be required and would be returned to the 
Commercial Developer. The negotiated assurances include:
o
A deposit of $3 million at building permit issuance for the garage, as described in the September 16, 2024, City 
Council study session; and  
o
An additional deposit of $3.5 million at certificate of occupancy (C of O) of the commercial component, as 
described in the September 16, 2024, City Council study session; and  
o
Should the Residential Developer be unable to proceed, the Commercial Developer will find an alternate 
affordable housing partner, with City’s approval, who can revise and deliver the affordable housing component 
within a to-be-determined prescribed timeframe; and  
o
All plans and entitlements to revert to the City to find an affordable developer who can deliver the residential 
units or explore alternatives such as a market-rate project.   
•
The project will be subject to prevailing wage and Department of Industrial Relations (DIR) registry requirements.
•
The shared parking will include a minimum of 37 reserved residential spaces, 25 commercial spaces shared with 
residential; and the additional spaces in the commercial garage will be publicly accessible on evenings and 
weekends. 
•
The Development Team shall make a good faith effort to propose a joint public art proposal for the residential 
and commercial components of the project at the project site. 
•
A 10-year DA/DDLA term was the original timeline described in the proposal when the ENA was first executed. 
However, due to the applicants’ proposed Schedule of Performance, including over three years’ lead time before 
breaking ground for the garage and multiple rounds of funding applications, this term will be longer. Project 
timing was discussed at the September 16, 2024, City Council study session and is explained further below under 
Project Schedule of Performance.  
Project Schedule of Performance
While the ENA outlined a 10-year overall timeline, the Development Team originally proposed 14 years for completion, 
with a four-year timeline to submit building permit applications, the first step in the process. This proposed schedule and 
the ability to receive building permits for the residential component within the current Housing Element Cycle, which 
concludes in 2031, were key discussion points in the September 2024 City Council study session. As a comparison, the Kiku 
Crossing 100 percent affordable housing project with 225 units and a parking garage with over 525 spaces, was entitled 
and built within four years. 
In September 2024, the City Council, directed staff to draft a project Schedule of Performance (Schedule of Performance) 
159 of 570

--- page 160 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
that provides time to the Residential Developer for multiple rounds of financing, as outlined in the timeline proposed by 
the Development Team. That timeline anticipated that the housing units receive building permits by 2031, to align with the 
current Housing Element Cycle. The attached draft Schedule of Performance (Attachment 6) lays out completion dates for 
each of the project’s key milestones, accounting for upfront delays in the project schedule for submittal and issuance of 
building permits (over 3years) and the cumulative effect of allowable extensions to those key milestones in the Schedule 
of Performance, referred to as buffers. Without buffers, the residential project would be completed by January 2034, 
assuming entitlements are obtained by May 2025. If the project utilized all stated buffers, the residential portion would 
not be completed until September 2039. It should be noted, however, that the Schedule of Performance must be adjusted 
based on the effective entitlement date, which remains to be determined; all dates will shift accordingly in the final 
version.
Staff is seeking Council approval of the amendment to extend the ENA to October 2025. If approved, staff will continue to 
work towards finalizing the required legal documents for the project following the key deal points outlined above and will 
bring them back to Council for final review and approval as part of the overall development application.  The Planning 
Commission and City Council public hearings for this project are anticipated later this spring or early summer of this year.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Depending on Council direction regarding the ENA extension and potential project entitlements, the City would be 
committing its parcel to this project for a 99-year ground lease at $1 per year and providing $3 million to support the 
affordable housing development. This project will be subject to all of the City’s development impact fees, including but not 
limited to, traffic impact fees, commercial-linkage fees, and park impact fees. The residential project would pay the impact 
fees at occupancy due to recent state legislation (SB 937), but the Commercial Developer would be required to pay the full 
in-lieu and impact fees prior to building permit issuance. 
Substantive staff time and cost has also been spent on the review and development of the legal agreements, which are 
accounted for in individual Department budgets. Staff and technical environmental consultant time on the entitlement will 
be covered by the Commercial Developer, although the Development Team has proposed drawing from the $2.9 million 
Commercial Developer contribution to reimburse 1/3rd of some costs associated with entitlement of the project. The 
reimbursement of these costs is still under discussion at the time of this report. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical change in 
the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
NOTICE PROVIDED:
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

 EXCLUSIVE NEGOTIATING RIGHTS AGREEMENT  
DOWNTOWN SAN MATEO AFFORDABLE HOUSING SITE 
This Exclusive Negotiating Rights Agreement (“Agreement”) is entered into as of 
_________________, 2021 (“Effective Date”) by and between the City of San Mateo, a 
California municipal corporation (“City”), and DTSM Housing, LLC, a Delaware limited liability 
company (“Developer”).  The City and the Developer are collectively referred to herein as 
the “Parties” and sometimes individually as a “Party”.  
RECITALS 
A.
The City owns or controls certain real property located at 4th and Railroad
Avenue, as more particularly described in Exhibit A attached to this Agreement (the “Site” or the 
“Property”). 
B.
On January 7, 2021, the City issued a Notice of Availability of Surplus Land to
solicit proposals to develop on the Site an affordable housing development consistent with the 
California Surplus Lands Act (California Government Code Section 54220-54234).  Developer 
was selected as developer of the Site.   
C.
Developer proposes to develop on the Site a mixed-use development project,
including below market housing (the “Project”), which Developer may propose to integrate into a 
larger development to include adjacent land owned or leased by Developer.  
D.
The purpose of this Agreement is to establish: (1) the general tasks to be
undertaken by the Developer and City during the term of this Agreement; and (2) the procedures 
and standards for the negotiation by the Parties of a Disposition and Development Agreement 
(the "DDA"). As more fully set forth herein, the Parties acknowledge and agree that this 
Agreement in and of itself does not obligate either Party to acquire or convey any property, does 
not grant the Developer the right to develop the Project, does not obligate the City to approve 
any planning applications, and does not obligate either Party to engage in any activities or incur 
any costs to develop the Project other than those activities set forth herein. 
NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual covenants and promises contained 
herein and for other valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which is hereby 
acknowledged, the Parties mutually agree as follows:  
ARTICLE 1 EXCLUSIVE NEGOTIATING RIGHT 
Section 1.1 
Incorporation of Recitals.  The Recitals set forth above are hereby 
incorporated by reference and made part of this Agreement.  
Section 1.2 
Good Faith Negotiations. The City and the Developer shall negotiate 
diligently and in good faith, during the Negotiating Period described in Section 1.3, the terms of a 
DDA for the development of the Project. During the Negotiating Period, the parties shall use 
good faith efforts to accomplish the respective tasks outlined in Article 2 to facilitate the 
negotiation of a mutually satisfactory DDA. The City and Developer agree that the negotiations 
pursuant to this Agreement and the DDA will be based on the proposal received from the 
Developer on  May 27, 2021 (the “Proposal”), a copy of which is attached to this Agreement as

10/14/2021
162 of 570

--- page 163 ---
2 
Exhibit B for reference purposes. 
 
Section 1.3 
Negotiating Period.  
(a) 
The Negotiation Period (“Negotiation Period”) shall commence upon the 
date the City approves and executes this Agreement and continue until the earlier  of (i) 
thirty-six (36) months after the Effective Date, and (ii) date which is eighteen (18) months 
following City’s written notice that Developer’s Formal Entitlements Application, as set 
forth in the ENA Timeline (defined in Section 2.2, below), is substantially complete; 
provided that the Negotiation Period may be further extended as set forth below. 
 
(b) 
The Negotiation Period shall be extended or tolled as follows: 
 
(i) with the written consent of the Developer and the City’s City Manager, 
extended up to a total of six (6) months if, despite the diligent efforts of 
Developer, longer than anticipated processing times delay the achievement of the 
milestones set forth in the ENA Timeline.  
  
 
(ii) if (A) the zoning designation for the property associated with the 
Entitlements Application for the Project is changed due to a modification of the 
general plan that requires a significant revision to the Entitlements Application, or 
(B) the Entitlements Application for the Project requires an environmental impact 
report, good cause shall be deemed to exist and the Negotiation Period shall 
automatically be extended for a single twelve (12) month period.  If the events 
described in both (A) and (B) occur, a second non-automatic extension may be 
granted with the written consent of Developer and City Manager upon the City 
Manager’s determination that good cause exists in its reasonable discretion.  In 
no event shall more than two (2) 12-month extensions be granted pursuant to this 
subsection (ii). 
 
(iii) 
automatically extended for twelve (12) months upon the Planning 
Commission’s or the City Council’s request for significant modifications to the 
Entitlements Application at a public hearing; 
 
(iv) 
automatically extended for up to six (6) months upon the filing by 
any party of an administrative appeal of any City decision or approval related to 
the Project for the period of time it takes for the administrative appeal to be 
resolved; 
 
(v) 
automatically tolled for a period of up to three (3) years upon the 
filing of a referendum petition affecting any City decision or approval related to 
the Project or the filing by any party of a lawsuit relating to any City decision or 
approval related to the Project.  The tolling period shall end upon the date the 
referendum petition or litigation is resolved; 
  
(vi) 
tolled for the period of time that Developer’s compliance with the 
ENA Timeline is delayed, through no action or omission of Developer, by a Force 
Majeure Event. For purposes hereof, “Force Majeure Event” means (1) acts, 
failures to act and/or delays of the City in accordance with the ENA Timeline; (ii) 
acts of God; (iii) flood, fire, earthquake, explosion, pandemic; (iv) war, invasion, 
hostilities (whether war is declared or not), terrorist threats or acts, riot, or other 
civil unrest; (iv) national or regional emergency, or other event out of Developer’s 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 65FA6E34-B7E1-457C-BFAF-F138124E671A
163 of 570

--- page 164 ---
3 
reasonable control that impacts the ability of Developer or City to meet milestone 
deadlines as set forth in this Agreement.  The foregoing notwithstanding, any 
extension due to a Force Majeure Event shall run from the time of 
commencement of the Force Majeure Event, but only if written notice is delivered 
by Developer to City within thirty (30) days following such commencement of the 
cause of such Force Majeure Event, and shall continue for the period of time that 
such Force Majeure Event materially impacts Developer’s or City’s ability to 
comply with the ENA Timeline. 
As used in this Agreement, "Negotiation Period" will include any of the applicable 
above described extension or tolling periods.   
(c)
If the City has not approved the DDA by the expiration of the Negotiation
Period (as extended by operation of the preceding paragraph), then this Agreement shall 
automatically terminate and neither Party shall have any further rights or obligations 
under this Agreement, unless the City, in its sole discretion, agrees in writing to an 
extension.  If a DDA is approved by the City and executed by the Developer prior to the

Period, upon the execution of the DDA, this Agreement shall terminate, and all rights and

Section 1.4  Exclusive Negotiations. During the Negotiating Period, the City shall not 
negotiate with any entity, other than the Developer, regarding sale or development of the Site or 
any portion thereof, or solicit or entertain bids or proposals to do so. This provision shall not 
preclude the City from providing copies of documents or information related to the Site in 
response to a request under the California Public Records Act or other applicable statutory

ARTICLE 2. DISCLOSURES AND COOPERATION 
Section 2.1  Overview. During the Negotiating Period, the parties shall use reasonable 
good faith efforts to accomplish the pre-construction tasks set forth in this Article and to 
accomplish the negotiation of a mutually acceptable DDA. To facilitate negotiation of the DDA, 
the parties shall use reasonable good faith efforts to accomplish the tasks set forth in this Article 
in a timeframe that will support achievement of these goals.  
CITY and Developer shall generally cooperate with each other and supply such documents 
and information as may be reasonably requested by the other to facilitate the conduct of the 
negotiations. Each party shall be responsible for its own costs and expenses related to the 
negotiation of the DDA.  
Developer expressly acknowledges and agrees that neither City nor Developer will be 
bound by any statement, promise or representation made by City staff or Developer during the 
course of negotiations of a DDA and that the City and Developer shall be legally bound only upon 
the approval of the DDA by Developer and by the San Mateo City Council.   
Developer further acknowledges and agrees that nothing in this Agreement or City’s 
cooperation in satisfying its obligations under this Agreement shall be deemed a promise, 
representation or guaranty that the parties will reach any future agreement or enter into a DDA.

Section 2.3  Due Diligence.  
During the Negotiating Period the Developer shall conduct due diligence activities, 
including but not limited to planning, soils report, hazardous materials report, financial feasibility 
and title adequacy.  Developer shall conduct or cause its consultants or subcontractors to 
conduct physical due diligence activities to determine whether the Site is suitable for the 
Development, taking into account the geotechnical and soils conditions, the presence or 
absence of toxic or other hazardous materials, the massing of the proposed Development 
improvements and the parking requirements imposed on developments of this type and the 
other environmental and regulatory factors that the Developer deems relevant. Developer and 
City shall cooperate to timely secure any permission and noticing agreements needed for the 
Developer to conduct such physical due diligence activities at the Site. If, in the Developer's 
judgment based on such investigations and analyses, the Site is not suitable for development, 
the Developer may notify the City in writing prior to the expiration of the Negotiating Period of its 
determination (an "Unsuitability Notice"). Upon delivery by any of the methods described in 
Section 3.2 of an Unsuitability Notice by the Developer to the City, this Agreement shall be 
terminated without further action of any party, and thereafter no party shall have any further 
duties, obligations, rights, or liabilities under this Agreement.  The Parties acknowledge that any 
executed DDA shall provide a similar opportunity for the Developer to determine the physical 
suitability of the Site. 
In addition, within thirty (30) days following the date of this Agreement, the Developer 
shall cause a reputable title company to issue a Preliminary Title Report (the "Report") on the 
Site, and the Developer shall make a copy of the Report available to the City.  If the Developer 
objects to any exception appearing on the Report or should any title exception arise after the 
date of the Report, the Developer may object to such exception, provided such objection is 
made to City in writing within forty-five (45) days of receiving the Report. If the Developer objects 
to any exception to title, the City, within thirty (30) days of receipt of the Developer's objection 
shall notify the Developer in writing whether the City elects to (1) cause the exception to be 
removed of record, (2) obtain a commitment from the title company for an appropriate 
endorsement to the policy of title insurance to be issued to the Developer, insuring against the 
objectionable exception, or (3) terminate this Agreement unless the Developer elects to take title 
subject to such exception.  If any Party elects to terminate this Agreement pursuant to this 
Section 2.3, no Party shall thereafter have any obligations to or rights against the others 
hereunder. The Parties acknowledge that any executed DDA shall provide a similar opportunity 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 65FA6E34-B7E1-457C-BFAF-F138124E671A

6 
for the Developer to determine the title adequacy of the Site.

Environmental Determination.  It is the intent of the City and the

approvals; provided, that nothing in this Agreement shall be construed to compel the City to 
approve or make any particular findings with respect to any CEQA documentation.  The

cause preparation and consideration of any CEQA-required document, and shall otherwise

compel the City to approve or make any particular findings with respect to such CEQA 
documentation.  The Developer shall be responsible for the payment of all usual City fees and 
costs associated with the environmental review of the planning approvals and DDA. 
 
 
ARTICLE 3 GENERAL PROVISIONS  
Section 3.1 Limitation on Effect of Agreement. This Agreement shall not obligate either 
the City or the Developer to enter into a DDA or to enter into any particular Agreement. By 
execution of this Agreement, the City is not committing itself to any Project approvals or 
agreeing to undertake acquisition, disposition, or exercise of control over any Site or any portion 
of the Site. Execution of this Agreement by the City is merely an agreement to conduct a period 
of exclusive negotiations in accordance with the terms hereof, reserving for subsequent City 
Council action the final discretion and approval regarding the execution, if any, of an Agreement 
and all proceedings and decisions in connection therewith. In the event the parties do not 
execute a final DDA within the Negotiating Period and any extension agreed to in writing, this 
Agreement shall terminate and be of no further force and effect.  Any DDA resulting from 
negotiations pursuant to this Agreement shall become effective only if and after such DDA has 
been considered and approved by the City Council of the City, following conduct of all legally 
required procedures. 
Section 3.2  Governing Law and Venue.  The laws of the State of California govern the 
interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement.  In the event of litigation, venue will be in the 
County of San Mateo. 
 
Section 3.3  Indemnity.  Developer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the City,

parties that no lis pendens shall be filed against the Site, or any portion of the Site, with respect

Section 3.10 Costs and Expenses. Except as otherwise specified herein, each party

Section 3.11 Default and Remedies. 
(a) Default. Failure by either party to negotiate in good faith as provided in this
Agreement shall constitute an event of default hereunder. The non-defaulting party shall give 
written notice of a default to the defaulting party, specifying the nature of the default and the 
required action to cure the default. If a default remains uncured thirty (30) days after receipt by 
the defaulting party of such notice, or, if the default cannot be cured within thirty (30) days, if the 
defaulting party fails to commence to cure within thirty (30) days and thereafter diligently pursue 
such cure and complete such cure within a reasonable period thereafter, the non-defaulting 
party may exercise the remedies set forth in subsection (b).  
(b) Remedies. In the event of an uncured default by either party, the Developer may elect
the following remedies: (i) terminate this Agreement, in which case following such termination, 
neither Party shall have any further right, remedy or obligation under this Agreement; or (ii) seek 
specific performance of the good faith and exclusive negotiating obligations of the City under this 
Agreement. The Developer's remedy of specific performance shall mean that if the City 
breaches its duty of negotiating in good faith or negotiating exclusively with the Developer, that 
the Developer may seek appropriate order requiring the City to re-engage in meaningful and 
good faith negotiations and/or cease or refrain from negotiating with any third party until the end 
of the Negotiating Period.  In the event of an uncured default by the Developer, the City’s sole 
remedy shall be to terminate this Agreement. Following such termination, neither party shall 
have any further right, remedy or obligation under this Agreement; provided, however, each 
Party’s indemnification obligation shall survive such termination.  
Except as expressly provided above, neither party shall have any liability to the other for 
damages or otherwise for any default, nor shall either party have any other claims with respect 
to performance under this Agreement. Each party specifically waives and releases any such 
rights or claims they may otherwise have at law or in equity.  
Section 3.12 Assignment. The Developer may not transfer or assign any or all of its 
rights or obligations hereunder except with the prior written consent of the City, which consent 
shall be granted or withheld in the City's sole discretion, and any such attempted transfer or 
assignment without the prior written consent of the City shall be void. Notwithstanding the 
foregoing, the Developer shall have the right to assign its rights and obligations under this 
Agreement to an affiliated entity, pursuant to a form of assignment agreement approved in 
advance by the City. 
Section 3.13 No Third Party Beneficiaries. This Agreement is made and entered into 
solely for the benefit of the City and the Developer and no other person shall have any right of 
action under or by reason of this Agreement.

Section 3.16 Right of Entry.  The Developer and its consultants shall have the right to

company affiliations of any personnel that will perform such work, and a projected schedule for 
the work. Such work, once approved, shall be conducted during normal business hours, unless 
otherwise approved by the City.  In connection with such entry and investigation, the Developer 
shall: (i) give the City or its designee reasonable advance notice; (ii) repair and restore any 
damage it may cause; (iii) carry or cause its consultants entering the site to maintain insurance 
in accordance with Section 3.21, including without limitation the obligation to obtain an 
endorsement to have the City named as an additional insured; and (iv) deliver to the City, within 
ten (10) days of receipt thereof, a complete copy of any investigation, test, report or study which 
the Developer conducts, or causes to be conducted, with respect to the Site.   
Section 3.17 Non-Recourse to Agents.  No member, official, employee, agent, or 
consultant of any Party to this Agreement shall be personally liable to any other Party, or any 
successor in interest or person claiming by, through or under any Party, in the event of any 
default or breach, or for or on account of any amount which may be or become due, or in any 
claim, cause or obligation whatsoever under the terms of this Agreement.   
Section 3.18 Relationship of the Parties. The subject of this Agreement is a co-
development undertaking between the Parties for coordinated development of the Site.  Aside 
from the relationship between the Parties described herein and to be further negotiated under 
this Agreement for inclusion in the executed DDA, neither Party is acting as the agent of the 
other Party in any respect, and none of the provisions in this Agreement shall be deemed to 
render the City a partner in any additional business, venture, or enterprise of the Developer. 
Section 3.19 Cooperation.  In connection with this Agreement, the Developer and the 
City shall reasonably cooperate with one another to achieve the objectives and purposes of this 
Agreement. In so doing, the Parties shall each refrain from doing anything that would render its 
performance under this Agreement impossible, and each shall do everything that this Agreement 
contemplates that the Party shall do to accomplish the purchase of this Agreement.

May 27, 2021

RE: 
Notice of Availability of Surplus Land – Response to Follow-up Proposal Parameters 
 
APNs: 034-179-050; 034-179-060 
 
Dear Ms. Kleinbaum: 
Thank you for the opportunity to respond to your questions and comments as described in the “Surplus 
Land’s Act Follow-up Proposal Parameters,” related to the surplus land owned by the City of San Mateo, 
located at the corner of S. Railroad Avenue and E. 4th Avenue in Downtown San Mateo (“Talbots City 
Lot”).   
 
Please consider the below response as supplemental information to the original proposal submitted on 
March 8, 2021, and the response to follow-up questions, provided on April 5, 2021.  As previously 
expressed, the Harvest Properties, Prometheus and Alta Housing team remain enthusiastic and 
dedicated to working collaboratively with you, your staff, and all City of San Mateo departments to 
advance the objectives and priorities of the City with respect to the site, which offers a unique 
opportunity to create a much needed supply of affordable housing for San Mateo’s residents as well as 
energize and beautify this part of Downtown San Mateo. 
 
In the following narrative, you will find a detailed response to each question posed.  Before delving into 
these details, we wanted to highlight four key elements or components of our proposed plan for your 
review and consideration.   
 
Project Vision:  Alignment with City Objectives 
Our development team is committed to providing a 100% affordable housing project on the Talbots City 
Lot that will feature a range of unit sizes from studios to 3-bedroom units, and will maximize the number 
of units that the site can accommodate in a 7-story project.  We understand that the City wants the 
project to accommodate a wide mix of income levels and age ranges, and can assure you that we share 
this as a critical and essential priority. 
 
As we mentioned in our April response letter to the City, if a Seniors residential community were the 
preferred direction, based on a unit mix that includes all studio and 1-bedroom units, the preliminary 
design for the site yields approximately 80 units of housing. This is a viable option that our development 
team would be very interested and open to pursuing in collaboration with the City.  We are equally 
committed to developing a Family-oriented project, if the City opted to move in that direction.  Based on 
our current design and yield analysis, a Family-oriented project would generate 52-54 housing units, and 
house 138 residents (according to HUD guidelines).  In this development scenario, the unit mix would 
consist of over 50% of the units as two and three-bedrooms and the income levels would range from 
30% to 80% AMI, with an average income level of approximately 43%. 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 65FA6E34-B7E1-457C-BFAF-F138124E671A
174 of 570

--- page 175 ---
Our team has a shared commitment to provide our residents of all ages with services which are 
resident-drive, strength-based, impactful, and empowering.  As you can see in the attached Site Plan 
Exhibit, located on the second story (at the podium level), our plan will include interior and exterior 
amenity areas for the residents to use and enjoy including a tot lot for children.  While the programming 
and design details have not yet been developed, we anticipate that these spaces including community 
space on the ground level will be utilized for a full range of family-oriented activities, including but not 
limited to: flexible spaces for after-school educational programs, play equipment and areas for children 
to exercise and socialize, food security and health and wellness programs, and recreational space for the 
community to meet and gather.  
 
Additionally, at the ground level, there will be a welcoming entrance and lobby area for families to enter 
the building and feel a sense of privacy and comfort.  Again, while the specific details and interior space 
planning will be developed over time with the input of the community and the City, we anticipate that 
this area will include a welcome desk, resident mailbox area, seating areas, package room, and a 
community notice or bulletin board, and community gathering area. The ground floor retail area will be 
leased long-term to Self-Help for the Elderly, which will include a large multi-purpose area. Given that a 
Family-oriented project generally has most of its events in the late afternoon and evening, the 
development team hopes to work with Self-Help for the Elderly to allow the residential community to 
have controlled access to this space during non-business hours.  
 
Self-Help for the Elderly 
On the ground floor of the proposed plan, our development team is committed to providing space to 
house, the non-profit Self-Help for the Elderly.  We had the great pleasure of meeting with Ms. Anni 
Chung, its President and CEO. We applaud the work of this non-profit organization, that has tirelessly 
strived and succeeded in serving seniors in the community for over 50 years.  We would be delighted to 
work with Self-Help for the Elderly to support its efforts towards improving the quality of life for older 
adults by empowering themselves and providing a comprehensive range of multicultural and 
multilingual services.   
 
Our current site plan has approximately 5,000 sq. ft. of ground floor community-serving space.  In our 
meeting with Ms. Chung, she shared more about Self-Help for the Elderly’s specific space needs for their 
programs.  Specifically, they need office space for health care-related supportive and administrative 
services, a small prep kitchen, as well as a multi-purpose room that can be used to provide meal service 
(65-75 people), classroom/workshop space, and performance and recreational uses. Based on these 
requirements, the development team will be able to meet all of their needs to allow them to continue 
providing the desired services for the community at the project.  
 
Additionally, since our team has site control of the adjacent property at S. B. Street, we believe we can 
expand the development footprint, and can provide an even more robust set of community benefits, 
which could also include additional ground floor space for Self-Help for the Elderly to ensure that their 
current and future needs could be satisfied.  We understand the limited resources and financial 
constraints of this non-profit organization and their importance to the whole San Mateo community.  
Our team plans to provide this space at an extremely below market rent.  The team will work with Self-
Help for the Elderly to ensure an appropriate build-out that meets their vision for the space. In our 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 65FA6E34-B7E1-457C-BFAF-F138124E671A
175 of 570

--- page 176 ---
discussions with Self-Help for the Elderly, between our delivery condition and their reserved funds, we 
collectively will be able to provide the required capital for them to successfully move into the project.  
 
Outside Sources of Funding 
Our development team understands the City’s proposed structure, which consists of the City’s 
contribution of the Talbots City Lot, in the form of a long-term ground lease for $1 per year. That said, 
the development team prefers a fee simple transfer, but can accommodate the ground lease structure, 
if preferred by the City.   Additionally, we fully appreciate the importance of minimizing the City’s 
financial assistance to the maximum extent possible by applying for multiple outside sources of funding.   
In response to this City priority, we have explored, researched and analyzed the outside sources as 
detailed in the “Follow-up Proposal Parameters”, and have determined that a Family-oriented project of 
our size and scope would qualify for and be able to obtain these outside funding sources.   
 
Project Labor Agreement  
The development team shares the City’s priority for supporting local Labor Unions, wherever possible.  
The Partnership is open to exploring a potential Project Labor Agreement for this project. In that spirit, 
the project team has reached out to the SMC Building Trades Council to initiate that discussion and we 
look forward to continuing the dialogue on this topic.    
 
Below please find responses to the specific questions raised in Follow-up Proposal Parameters.  If you 
have any further questions or comments, our development team would be very happy to answer any 
questions you might have.  As we have stated in both our original proposal and our previous response to 
the City, in the end, our goal is to support the City in achieving its objectives of providing safe, secure 
and attractive affordable housing for its residents.    
 
We are confident that if the City were to choose our development team, you would find the Alta 
Housing, Prometheus and Harvest Properties team to be one that is collaborative, transparent, and 
always willing to have an open dialogue with you, all members of the City’s team, as well as community 
stakeholders.  Harvest and Prometheus are very proud to be working with Alta Housing on this 
exceptional community opportunity.  Alta Housing (previously Palo Alto Housing), a community-based 
non-profit has been developing and managing affordable housing in San Mateo and Santa Clara Counties 
for over 50 years.  They are uniquely positioned to manage and provide all the residential supportive 
care services that will be needed and required for the residents to thrive. 
 
Working collaboratively with Self Help for the Elderly, in conjunction with the City of San Mateo, we are 
committed to working together to create a new development project that serves the multi-generational 
needs of the San Mateo community.  Thank you again for this opportunity to respond to your questions.  
If you should have any further questions or require additional clarification, please do not hesitate to let 
us know.  Our development team truly looks forward to the next steps in the selection process.

City Question Responses:  
1) How many units do you propose to build? 
The project currently proposes 52-54-Family-oriented units including a mix of studios, one-bedroom, 
two-bedroom, and three-bedroom units. Please see below chart for a detailed breakdown of the 
unit mix. The Family-oriented project continues to be our recommended approach as the project 
scores very strong for many funding sources, caters to residents of all ages creating a multi-
generational project, and despite fewer total units, the project will accommodate 138-residents 
which is approximately 25% more residents than a 90-unit senior housing project. The development 
team has elected to provide a larger amenity area on the second floor that creates an 
indoor/outdoor environment resulting in a 52-unit project, but if the City prefers the higher range of 
units then the development team can reduce the size of the indoor amenity area to add two 
additional units, resulting in a 54-unit project.  
 
 
 
2)  What income ranges would your project accommodate? 
The project provides units ranging from 30% to 80% AMI with an average income level of 
approximately 43%.  
 
3)  How much on-site parking are you able to provide for the residential units? 
The project currently proposes 90 total parking stalls across the site. The current plan for the Family-
oriented housing project is to allocate 0.7 stalls per unit (37-stalls) to the residential portion of the 
project. The four different uses at the project have different peak demand times. The goal will be to 
incorporate a shared parking concept, as is standard in Downtown San Mateo projects, among the 
different uses: i) residential, ii) commercial, iii) retail, and iv) public parking. This shared concept will 
provide excess parking stalls to the residential units during off-peak hours.  If additional parcels on 
the block are acquired, then it will allow for an increased below-grade parking structure providing 
the opportunity for additional stalls to be allocated to the residential as well as additional flex 
parking for the residential and the public during off-peak commercial times.   
 
4)  Describe any on-site amenities associated with your project.  
The project will dedicate the majority of the ground floor to Self-Help for the Elderly to provide a 
permanent location for the organization so it can continue to serve the needs of the community of 
San Mateo.  Self-Help for the Elderly’s space will consist of a multi-faceted large community room to 
serve daily lunches, host dancing/music and other recreational events, as well as allow for the 
option of partitioning the space into three classroom areas for educational workshops. The 
development team also plans to work with Self-Help for the Elderly so that some of this space may 
be available to the residential community in the evenings and on the weekends.  We see these two 
uses as being very complimentary to each other.

177 of 570

--- page 178 ---
The second floor of the project will provide 1,500 square feet of indoor space and over 2,500 square 
feet of outdoor space to create an indoor/outdoor amenity area for the residents. This space has 
not been fully programmed but will consist of flexible spaces for after-school activities and 
homework, play equipment and areas for children to exercise and socialize, and recreational space 
for the community to meet and gather. 
 
The development team is committed to developing a community where residents not only live but 
thrive.  Alta Housing has a long track record of providing on-site supportive services to its residential 
communities.  Alta Housing’s mission has been to partner with residents to provide services which 
are resident-driven, strength-based, impactful, and empowering.  Alta currently provides support in 
the following areas: 1) Supporting Counseling & Housing Retention to help residents meet their 
goals, 2) Community Building & Resident Leadership, and 3) Educational Programs & Social Services 
such as after-school and summer youth programs, parenting classes, financial literacy workshops, 
job coaching, and health and wellness initiatives.  
 
5) What is your expectation of the required ask of the City’s affordable housing fund? 
The revised proposal requests $2,000,000 of funding from the City of San Mateo. The development 
team will exhaust all potential avenues of funding prior to relying on the City for any financial 
commitment. The current proposal does not include the Commercial Linkage fee from the 
development team’s commercial projects. The Commercial Linkage fee will be paid directly to the 
City for each project. The Commercial Linkage fee will increase the City’s affordable housing fund by 
over 50%, providing the ability for the City to apply these funds to additional housing opportunities, 
thereby helping to address the housing shortage within the Bay Area. Based on current available 
funding sources, we estimate the $5.6M of Commercial Linkage fees could be leveraged to create an 
additional 20-30 housing units for the community.  
 
The $5.6M of proposed Commercial Linkage fees that will be paid to the City includes $1,300,000 of 
additional fees that is directly related to the Talbots City Lot that the City would not obtain if the 
team is unsuccessful in acquiring a lease-hold interest in the City Lot. Additionally, the development 
team will contribute equity up to $2,900,000 as a Developer Community Benefit Contribution to the 
project to cover any potential shortfall and provide for greater certainty of execution.   
 
6) What other funding sources will you seek for this project? 
With funding for affordable housing currently and projected to be in very short supply, our team 
worked diligently and engaged a third-party expert to confirm the potential competitiveness of the 
full range of state, federal, and other housing funding programs (see table below).  In this way and 
combined with our own Developer Community Benefit Contribution, we aim to minimize and 
potentially eliminate financial support necessary from the City. Please see below the competitive 
funding scoring matrix developed for our project:

*The lowest score among the projects which were successfully awarded funding in that program’s 
previous round inclusive of the set-aside, if applicable, in which this project would compete. 
Furthermore, our due diligence confirms that the proposed project design and location meets the 
current regulatory requirements necessary to compete as a family project in a High Resource Area, 
giving it a distinct competitive advantage to all other projects across the State which do not meet 
these requirements. 
 
At the same time, our financing plan takes into account that two sources which continue to be 
challenging or out-of-reach for even the best family projects, namely Section 8 vouchers and 9% tax 
credits.  
• 
For Section 8, our due diligence confirmed that currently project-based vouchers are 
oversubscribed with limited availability except for an allocation for veterans. We will continue to 
stay in contact with the County should that change. 
• 
The 9% tax credit program continues to be limited due to the size of the Region. That said, we 
are looking at maximizing the adaptability to be competitive for 9% and understand that HCD is 
exploring the possibility of allowing the MHP program to be used with 9% tax credits; this is 
currently disallowed.  Were that to happen, we would apply our full effort to achieve the 
9%/MHP combo, in order to reach the above-stated goal of reducing or eliminating City funding. 
 
Funding Sources Overview: 
Our projections effectively and fully leverage State funding and other sources – including the Developer 
Community Benefits Contribution – to minimize the City’s financial assistance.  A discussion of each 
source follows: 
a. 4% Tax Credit:  
The project assumes 4% tax credit funding with the competitive CDLAC tiebreaker necessary to 
obtain this funding.  
 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 65FA6E34-B7E1-457C-BFAF-F138124E671A
179 of 570

--- page 180 ---
b. San Mateo County Affordable Housing Fund (AHF):  
In order to limit the number of required sources, the proposal currently does not rely on funding 
from the County, but during the financing process the development team will leave “no stone 
left unturned” and will exhaust all potential funding sources. The Developers Community Benefit 
Contribution will supplant the customary necessary funding from the County of San Mateo.  
c. Federal Home Bank Affordable Housing Program (AHP): 
We have included $765,000 in AHP funding; per our research, the project surpasses the 
threshold required to be considered competitive for this funding source. Of note, a higher per-
unit AHP loan than what we are including significantly reduces application competitiveness 
under current regulations. 
d. Project Based Vouchers:  
Conversations with the County have confirmed that currently project-based Section 8 vouchers 
are very limited and prioritized for veterans. The Section 8 program is most often called upon to 
support the limited income available from tenants exiting homelessness. Rather than rely upon 
this scarce resource – and potentially delay project construction – we instead opted to leverage 
the higher incomes available from the 80% AMI units and supplement the shortfall with the 
Developers Community Benefit Contribution. However, it is important to note that this did not 
come at the expense of fully funding the service provision for the formerly homeless tenants 
which is a necessary component to help them stay housed; our budget includes 1 FTE Service 
Coordinator and 1 FTE Services Specialist. 
e. Other State Programs: 
a. Multi-Family Housing Program (MHP) 
Our project intentionally takes full advantage of MHP for the following reasons: 
1. It is routinely the most-funded State program with the greatest number of 
funding “rounds” per year with the greatest funding per round. 
2. We have designed the project to meet the CTCAC requirements for family 
housing. That, combined with the site’s location in a High Resource Area, 
allows the project to garner a greater per-unit MHP subsidy vs. if either of 
these were not the case. This advantage comes without damaging the 
project’s overall competitiveness for this resource. 
3. The project has a very competitive tiebreaker and we are confident in our 
ability to obtain this financing.  
b. Infill Infrastructure Grant (IIG) 
We propose to pair the MHP funding with the IIG program.  Our due diligence has 
confirmed that the project could score extremely well, based upon the updated 
regulations issued this month. The IIG program will be an excellent source to cover 
related construction-period costs such as utility connections and sidewalk 
improvements. Our IIG “self-score” shows a high degree of feasibility in obtaining this 
competitive resource. 
 
c. Transit Oriented Development Programs (TOD) 
Although our due diligence confirms that the project would score very well for TOD (290 
out of 300, whereas 265 was the minimum to be awarded in the previous round), we 
propose not to rely upon this funding source unless one of the following facts is 
changed: 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 65FA6E34-B7E1-457C-BFAF-F138124E671A
180 of 570

--- page 181 ---
1. The TOD Program isn’t reliably available. 
1. The previous round in 2020 was the first TOD “NOFA” since 2014. 
2. HCD has not announced plans for another TOD NOFA. 
3. In addition, TOD funding often lags other programs ($140 million for 
TOD in 2020 vs. $160 million for IIG in 2021, $195 million for MHP in 
2021, and $297 million for MHP in 2020). 
2. HCD guidelines prohibit “subsidy stacking” – one unit cannot benefit from 
both MHP and TOD funding (but IIG is exempt). 
3. The greater per-unit loan amount available to this project under the MHP 
program – described above – is not available under TOD for any project; 
therefore, the maximum possible award under TOD will always be less than 
the maximum MHP award for this project under current regulations. 
 
 
7. Would you consider trying to acquire the parcels immediately fronting the Talbot’s parking lot 
along B-Street for a larger development site? 
The development team is open to acquiring the parcels fronting the Talbot’s parking lot. The 
opportunity to transform the full City block will lead to a very exciting development that will 
further allow the team to curate a marque project that meets the City’s vision for the 
Downtown area. Acquiring the adjacent sites will provide optimal site flexibility, additional 
parking stalls, enhanced architectural design, increased programming to activate the public 
realm, and provide subsidized rent to offer community serving retail. The team currently 
controls the 445 S. B Street (former Talbots Toy Store), which provides many benefits to the City 
Lot such as: i) replacement of public parking, ii) on-site parking for the residential units, iii) 
Developer Community Benefit Contribution of $2.9M to cover the potential short-fall and 
provide a further the ability to provide subterranean parking. 
8. Would you consider entering into a Project Labor Agreement for this project? 
The development team is open to exploring a Project Labor Agreement for this project and has 
reached out to James Ruigomez at the SMC Building Trades Council to discuss the potential to work 
together on this project.
