Exhibit D 
Due Diligence Insurance Requirements 
 
Developer shall procure and maintain, and/or cause its consultants, or subcontractors to procure 
and maintain, for the duration of the contract, insurance against claims for injuries to persons or 
damage to property which may arise from or in connection with the performance of the work 
hereunder its agents, representatives, employees, consultants, or subcontractors.  With respect 
to CGL and Contractors Pollution Liability, as they relate to an environmental contractor or 
consultant, coverage should be maintained for a minimum of five (5) years after contract 
completion. 
 
MINIMUM SCOPE AND LIMIT OF INSURANCE  
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office Form CG 00 01 covering 
CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products and completed operations, property 
damage, bodily injury and personal & advertising injury with limits no less than $2,000,000 per 
occurrence. If a general aggregate limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply 
separately to this project/location (ISO CG 25 03 or 25 04) or the general aggregate limit shall 
be twice the required occurrence limit.   
2. Automobile Liability:  Insurance Services Office Form Number CA 0001 covering any auto 
(Code 1), or if Contractor has no owned autos, hired (Code 8) and non-owned (Code 9) autos, 
with limit no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage. 
3. Workers’ Compensation insurance as required by the State of California, with Statutory 
Limits, and Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident 
for bodily injury or disease.  
4. Contractors Pollution Legal Liability Insurance.  If any of the work will involve physical 
testing or invasive investigation for hazardous materials, Developer’s contractor shall maintain 
in force, for the full period of such work, insurance covering losses caused by pollution 
conditions that arise from the operations of the contractor.  The policy of insurance affording 
these required coverages shall be written in an amount of at least $2,000,000 per loss, with 
an annual aggregate limit of at least $2,000,000 per policy period of one year.  
 
If the Developer or consultant or subcontractor maintains broader coverage and/or higher limits 
than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall be entitled to the broader coverage 
and/or the higher limits maintained by such entity. Any available insurance proceeds in excess 
of the specified minimum limits of insurance and coverage shall be available to the City. 
Self-Insured Retentions 
Self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. At the option of the City, 
the Developer its consultant or subcontractor shall provide coverage to reduce or eliminate such 
self-insured retentions as respects the City, its officers, officials, employees, and volunteers; or 
the Contractor shall provide evidence satisfactory to the City guaranteeing payment of losses and 
related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses.  
DocuSign Envelope ID: 65FA6E34-B7E1-457C-BFAF-F138124E671A
189 of 570

--- page 190 ---
16 
Other Insurance Provisions 
A. The General Liability, Automobile Liability and Contractors Pollution Liability policies are to 
contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
1. The City, its officers, officials, employees, and volunteers are to be covered as 
additional insureds with respect to liability arising out of work or operations performed by 
or on behalf of the Developer including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations by obtaining endorsement ISO Form CG 20 42, 
Additional Insured – Automatic Status for Designated Operations or equivalent. General 
liability coverage can be provided in the form of an endorsement to the Contractor’s 
insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, CG 11 85 or both CG 20 10, CG 20 
26, CG 20 33, or CG 20 38; and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used). 
2. For any claims related to this project, the insurance obtained by Developer and its 
consultants and subcontractors shall be primary insurance coverage at least as 
broad as ISO CG 20 01 04 13 as respects the City, its officers, officials, employees, agents, 
and volunteers. Any insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its officers, 
officials, employees, agents, or volunteers shall be excess of the Contractor’s insurance 
and shall not contribute with it. 
3. Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, 
except with notice to the City. 
B. If the work of the Developer or its consultants or subcontractors includes the transportation of 
hazardous materials, the Automobile Liability policy shall be endorsed to include 
Transportation Pollution Liability insurance, covering materials to be transported by Developer 
or its consultant or subcontractors pursuant to the contract. This coverage may also be 
provided on the Contractors Pollution Liability policy. 
C. If General Liability or Contractors Pollution Liability coverages are written on a claims-made 
form: 
1. The retroactive date must be shown and must be before the date of the contract or the 
beginning of contract work. 
2. Insurance must be maintained and evidence of insurance must be provided for at least five 
(5) years after completion of the contract of work. 
3. If coverage is canceled or non-renewed, and not replaced with another claims-made policy 
form with a retroactive date prior to the contract effective date, the Contractor must 
purchase an extended period coverage for a minimum of five (5) years after completion of 
contract work. 
4. A copy of the claims reporting requirements must be submitted to the City for review. 
 
Acceptability of Insurers 
Insurance is to be placed with insurers authorized to conduct business in the state with a current 
A.M. Best rating of no less than A:VII if admitted in the State of California.  
 
Verification of Coverage 
Contractor shall furnish the City with original Certificates of Insurance including all required 
amendatory endorsements (or copies of the applicable policy language effecting coverage 
required by this clause) and a copy of the Declarations and Endorsement Page of the CGL policy 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 65FA6E34-B7E1-457C-BFAF-F138124E671A
190 of 570

--- page 191 ---
17 
listing all policy endorsements to City before work begins. However, failure to obtain the required 
documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the Contractor’s obligation to provide them.  
The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all required insurance policies, 
including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.   
 
Waiver of Subrogation 
Contractor hereby grants to City a waiver of subrogation which any insurer may acquire against 
City, its officers, officials, employees, and volunteers, from Contractor by virtue of the payment of 
any loss. Contractor agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to affect this waiver 
of subrogation but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a 
waiver of subrogation endorsement from the insurer.   
The Workers’ Compensation policy shall be endorsed with a waiver of subrogation in favor of the 
City for all work performed by the Contractor, its employees, agents, and subcontractors. 
 
Subcontractors 
Contractor shall require and verify that all subcontractors maintain insurance meeting all the 
requirements stated herein, and Contractor shall ensure that City is an additional insured on 
insurance required from subcontractors. For CGL coverage subcontractors shall provide coverage 
with a format least as broad as CG 20 38 04 13. 
 
Special Risks or Circumstances 
City reserves the right to modify these requirements, including limits, based on the nature of the 
risk, prior experience, insurer, coverage, or other special circumstances.

CONSUMER DISCLOSURE
From time to time, Carahsoft OBO City of San Mateo (we, us or Company) may be required by
law to provide to you certain written notices or disclosures. Described below are the terms and
conditions for providing to you such notices and disclosures electronically through the
DocuSign, Inc. (DocuSign) electronic signing system. Please read the information below
carefully and thoroughly, and if you can access this information electronically to your
satisfaction and agree to these terms and conditions, please confirm your agreement by clicking
the ‘I agree’ button at the bottom of this document. 
Getting paper copies
At any time, you may request from us a paper copy of any record provided or made available
electronically to you by us. You will have the ability to download and print documents we send
to you through the DocuSign system during and immediately after signing session and, if you
elect to create a DocuSign signer account, you may access them for a limited period of time
(usually 30 days) after such documents are first sent to you. After such time, if you wish for us to
send you paper copies of any such documents from our office to you, you will be charged a
$0.00 per-page fee. You may request delivery of such paper copies from us by following the
procedure described below. 
Withdrawing your consent 
If you decide to receive notices and disclosures from us electronically, you may at any time
change your mind and tell us that thereafter you want to receive required notices and disclosures
only in paper format. How you must inform us of your decision to receive future notices and
disclosure in paper format and withdraw your consent to receive notices and disclosures
electronically is described below. 
Consequences of changing your mind
If you elect to receive required notices and disclosures only in paper format, it will slow the
speed at which we can complete certain steps in transactions with you and delivering services to
you because we will need first to send the required notices or disclosures to you in paper format,
and then wait until we receive back from you your acknowledgment of your receipt of such
paper notices or disclosures. To indicate to us that you are changing your mind, you must
withdraw your consent using the DocuSign ‘Withdraw Consent’ form on the signing page of a
DocuSign envelope instead of signing it. This will indicate to us that you have withdrawn your
consent to receive required notices and disclosures electronically from us and you will no longer
be able to use the DocuSign system to receive required notices and consents electronically from
us or to sign electronically documents from us. 
All notices and disclosures will be sent to you electronically
Unless you tell us otherwise in accordance with the procedures described herein, we will provide
electronically to you through the DocuSign system all required notices, disclosures,
authorizations, acknowledgements, and other documents that are required to be provided or
made available to you during the course of our relationship with you. To reduce the chance of
you inadvertently not receiving any notice or disclosure, we prefer to provide all of the required
notices and disclosures to you by the same method and to the same address that you have given
us. Thus, you can receive all the disclosures and notices electronically or in paper format through
the paper mail delivery system. If you do not agree with this process, please let us know as
described below. Please also see the paragraph immediately above that describes the
consequences of your electing not to receive delivery of the notices and disclosures

How to contact Carahsoft OBO City of San Mateo:
You may contact us to let us know of your changes as to how we may contact you electronically,
to request paper copies of certain information from us, and to withdraw your prior consent to
receive notices and disclosures electronically as follows:
 To contact us by email send messages to: cravi@cityofsanmateo.org
To advise Carahsoft OBO City of San Mateo of your new e-mail address 
To let us know of a change in your e-mail address where we should send notices and disclosures
electronically to you, you must send an email message to us at cravi@cityofsanmateo.org and in
the body of such request you must state: your previous e-mail address, your new e-mail address.  
We do not require any other information from you to change your email address..  
In addition, you must notify DocuSign, Inc. to arrange for your new email address to be reflected
in your DocuSign account by following the process for changing e-mail in the DocuSign system. 
To request paper copies from Carahsoft OBO City of San Mateo
To request delivery from us of paper copies of the notices and disclosures previously provided
by us to you electronically, you must send us an e-mail to cravi@cityofsanmateo.org and in the
body of such request you must state your e-mail address, full name, US Postal address, and
telephone number. We will bill you for any fees at that time, if any.
To withdraw your consent with Carahsoft OBO City of San Mateo
To inform us that you no longer want to receive future notices and disclosures in electronic
format you may:
i. decline to sign a document from within your DocuSign session, and on the subsequent
page, select the check-box indicating you wish to withdraw your consent, or you may;
ii. send us an e-mail to cravi@cityofsanmateo.org and in the body of such request you
must state your e-mail, full name, US Postal Address, and telephone number. We do not
need any other information from you to withdraw consent..  The consequences of your
withdrawing consent for online documents will be that transactions may take a longer time
to process..

CONSUMER DISCLOSURE
From time to time, Carahsoft OBO City of San Mateo (we, us or Company) may be required by
law to provide to you certain written notices or disclosures. Described below are the terms and
conditions for providing to you such notices and disclosures electronically through the
DocuSign, Inc. (DocuSign) electronic signing system. Please read the information below
carefully and thoroughly, and if you can access this information electronically to your
satisfaction and agree to these terms and conditions, please confirm your agreement by clicking
the ‘I agree’ button at the bottom of this document. 
Getting paper copies
At any time, you may request from us a paper copy of any record provided or made available
electronically to you by us. You will have the ability to download and print documents we send
to you through the DocuSign system during and immediately after signing session and, if you
elect to create a DocuSign signer account, you may access them for a limited period of time
(usually 30 days) after such documents are first sent to you. After such time, if you wish for us to
send you paper copies of any such documents from our office to you, you will be charged a
$0.00 per-page fee. You may request delivery of such paper copies from us by following the
procedure described below. 
Withdrawing your consent 
If you decide to receive notices and disclosures from us electronically, you may at any time
change your mind and tell us that thereafter you want to receive required notices and disclosures
only in paper format. How you must inform us of your decision to receive future notices and
disclosure in paper format and withdraw your consent to receive notices and disclosures
electronically is described below. 
Consequences of changing your mind
If you elect to receive required notices and disclosures only in paper format, it will slow the
speed at which we can complete certain steps in transactions with you and delivering services to
you because we will need first to send the required notices or disclosures to you in paper format,
and then wait until we receive back from you your acknowledgment of your receipt of such
paper notices or disclosures. To indicate to us that you are changing your mind, you must
withdraw your consent using the DocuSign ‘Withdraw Consent’ form on the signing page of a
DocuSign envelope instead of signing it. This will indicate to us that you have withdrawn your
consent to receive required notices and disclosures electronically from us and you will no longer
be able to use the DocuSign system to receive required notices and consents electronically from
us or to sign electronically documents from us. 
All notices and disclosures will be sent to you electronically
Unless you tell us otherwise in accordance with the procedures described herein, we will provide
electronically to you through the DocuSign system all required notices, disclosures,
authorizations, acknowledgements, and other documents that are required to be provided or
made available to you during the course of our relationship with you. To reduce the chance of
you inadvertently not receiving any notice or disclosure, we prefer to provide all of the required
notices and disclosures to you by the same method and to the same address that you have given
us. Thus, you can receive all the disclosures and notices electronically or in paper format through
the paper mail delivery system. If you do not agree with this process, please let us know as
described below. Please also see the paragraph immediately above that describes the
consequences of your electing not to receive delivery of the notices and disclosures

AGENDA ITEM
2.
4th and Railroad Parking Lot – Bespoke Development Term Sheet Options
Review and provide direction on the proposed Term Sheet options and possible scenarios for the Bespoke public-

Avenue.
CEQA: This study session to discuss deal terms is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with
certainty that it will not cause a physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.) The
proposed Bespoke development project will be subject to environmental review which is being completed through
the entitlement process.

--- page 215 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  2
Section Name: STUDY SESSION
Account Number: 26-3111
File ID: 24-9056
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
September 16, 2024 
SUBJECT:
4th and Railroad Parking Lot – Bespoke Development Term Sheet Options
RECOMMENDATION:
Review and provide direction on the proposed Term Sheet options and possible scenarios for the Bespoke public-private 
partnership development project, which includes the City-owned parking lot at 4th and Railroad Avenue.
 
BACKGROUND:
The City is currently a party to an Exclusive Negotiating Agreement (ENA) for the redevelopment of the City-owned surface 
parking lot located at 4th and Railroad Avenue adjacent to the parcel where the former Talbot’s building is located. The 
ENA is with a three-party development team comprised of Prometheus, Harvest Properties, and Alta Housing (a non-profit 
affordable housing developer) and is intended to be in place throughout the entitlement phase of the project. The City 
entered into the ENA in October 2021 (Attachment 1) and has since been working with the development team to refine 
the project, now referred to as Bespoke, through the planning entitlement process. The ENA is set to expire on October 14, 
2024 but may be extended for six months by mutual agreement between the Developer and the City Manager, who was 
delegated this authority by the City Council (ENA Section 1.3(b)(1)). 
The Bespoke project completed the pre-application phase of its planning review and submitted their formal planning 
application in February 2023. The project was deemed complete on July 17, 2024 and the environmental analysis, in the 
form of an Initial Study/Mitigated Negative Declaration, is nearly ready for public review and comment. 
The Project includes a parcel owned by the City, which means the City is not merely acting in a regulatory capacity but is 
also acting in a proprietary capacity as a partner. As the project has evolved, so have the proposed deal terms related to 
the City’s provision of land and funding support for the affordable housing component of the project. The last deal terms 
considered by Council at a study session on May 15, 2023 are included for reference (Attachment 2). 
This report is intended to provide the City Council with an update on the project status and to get Council direction on 
possible options relating to the currently proposed deal terms and the ENA and whether to extend it by six months.  
Project History
The City Council adopted a resolution declaring the surface parking lot at East 4th Avenue and South Railroad Avenue, 
known as the Talbot’s parking lot, as “Surplus Land” at the January 4, 2021, City Council meeting. Following this 
declaration, staff released a Notice of Surplus Lands Availability to all housing developers that have registered with the 
State Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD). In response to this notice, the City received four letters 
of interest for the development of affordable housing on the site. 
215 of 570

--- page 216 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
The City requested that the interested parties provide proposals addressing the following criteria:
1.
Develop a 100% affordable housing project featuring a range of unit sizes from studios up to 3-bedrooms. 
Project should accommodate a mix of incomes and age ranges.
2.
Maximize the number of units that can be accommodated on the site by building a 7-story project.
3.
Provide a ground floor space that can be used by the non-profit Self-Help for the Elderly. It can be assumed 
that they can contribute some amount to rent, but not likely a market rent, such as would be charged to a 
retailer. They will also provide some funding for their own tenant improvements.
4.
The City will contribute the land for the project in the form of a long-term ground lease.
5.
Specify the expected ask of financial assistance for the affordable housing from the City. It is expected that the 
development team will minimize the City’s financial assistance to the best possible extent by applying for 
multiple outside sources of financing.
6.
Indicate if the development team would consider trying to acquire the parcels immediately fronting the 
Talbot’s parking lot along B Street for a larger development site.
The City received three responsive proposals based on these criteria. After going through an evaluation process, on July 
19, 2021, the City Council directed staff to negotiate an Exclusive Negotiating Agreement (ENA) with the 
Prometheus/Harvest Properties/Alta Housing team to explore the redevelopment of the City’s parking lot site. The City 
Council subsequently approved the ENA at their October 4, 2021 meeting. 
The development team was selected based on their proposal to develop the full block, removing awkward transition and 
existing easement issues; proposed mix of unit sizes that make the project family friendly; offering the highest ratio of on-
site parking for the housing; and serving income levels up to 70% of Area Median Income (AMI) which addresses the 
Council’s interest in serving both low- and middle-income households. A mixed-use project on this block would help re-
energize this part of B Street, would have synergy with the adjacent Draeger’s Market project, and would contribute to the 
economic vitality of the city and downtown.
The proposed Project includes the following components:  
•
A seven-story 71-unit 100% affordable housing building on the City’s lot with community space for Self-Help 
for the Elderly on the first floor,   
•
A six -story 156,000 sq-ft retail and office building,   
•
128 parking spaces on-site in an underground parking garage (of which 37 would be for residents' use and 25 
would be shared between commercial and residential uses), access would be on City lot; the remaining 
commercial garage would be accessible to the public on evenings and weekends,
•
A combination of at grade open space and roof top common areas, and   
•
Improvements in the public right-of-way area along all four street frontages.   
Key Deal Terms
In May 2023, additional draft deal terms were presented to Council (Attachment 2). The draft deal terms form the basis of 
the Development Agreement (DA)/Disposition and Development Loan Agreement (DDLA) that the City and developer 
would enter into at the time that the project receives its entitlements. These have since been refined further to reflect 
additional negotiations and details that are being included in the DA/DDLA. A summary of the key project terms are as 
follows:
•
The development team will enter into a 99-year ground lease and a reciprocal easement agreement for the 
City’s parcel at the rate of $1 per year. 
•
The City will contribute $3 million (m) to support the construction of the affordable housing from the City’s 
dedicated affordable housing funds. The specific source of funds from within the City’s housing funds is still to 
be determined. Alta has requested the partial use of these funds for predevelopment costs, should there be 
impact fees due at building permit issuance. 
216 of 570

--- page 217 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
•
The development team will contribute $2.9m to support the pre-development costs for the affordable housing 
building.
•
The development team will finance and construct the shared underground parking garage across the entire 
site and create separate pads for the development of the affordable housing building and the commercial 
building. The commercial developer will cover the full cost of the construction of the parking garage, including 
the mechanical lift spaces dedicated to the affordable housing.
•
The development team will provide ground floor space in the affordable housing building at a substantially 
subsidized rent (the proposal from Alta is $1 per year) to Self-Help for the Elderly to allow for their relocation 
from the Central Park Recreation Center, which will be necessitated by the implementation of the Central Park 
Master Plan.
•
In order to allow for the construction of the underground parking garage across parcel lines, the development 
team will use a lot tie agreement.
•
Due to the construction readiness requirements of affordable housing financing, the underground parking 
garage must commence construction prior to the affordable housing project applying for funding. As a result, 
the commercial component is likely to be completed prior to the housing. The length of this differential 
between the completion of the commercial and the affordable housing components of the project will depend 
on whether the affordable housing has to go through multiple cycles of funding requests in order to obtain 
construction funding from the typical affordable housing financing sources, including the federal tax credits. 
This deal term is explained further below under Assurances to See Units Built as it has spurred additional 
conversation about how to safeguard the City’s interest in the project, which is to ensure the housing units are 
built, preferably within the current Housing Element Cycle.
•
The project will be subject to prevailing wage and Department of Industrial Relations (DIR) registry 
requirements.
•
The shared parking will include a minimum of 37 reserved residential spaces, 25 shared residential and 
commercial spaces; and the commercial garage will be publicly accessible on evenings and weekends. The 
publicly accessible part is a recent add to the project based on other Downtown projects.
•
A 10-year DA/DDLA term (additional details provided below under Project Schedule).
The majority of these draft deal terms have remained constant. However, there has been progress and refinements 
associated with publicly accessible parking, the lease amounts for Self Help for the Elderly, and movement on assurances 
to getting the units built. The site plan and architectural design also went through several iterations to address building 
design, public realm improvements, trash pickup, utilities etc., which is typical of any major development project proposal. 
The environmental review is nearly complete and poised for release to enable public comment and input. 
Since May 2023, the negotiations have been challenging on two primary fronts. First, the developers had not agreed to 
ensure that the affordable housing units will actually be constructed if Alta fails to procure funding or encounters some 
other hindrance toward Project completion. Second, the development team had originally declined to fully commit to 
delivering the housing units within staff’s proposed specific and reasonable timeframe (i.e., this Housing Element Cycle). 
  
Assurances to See Units Built
Broadly, the October 2021 and May 2023 terms involved (1) City contribution of land for the parking structure and 
residential units and additional funding for the residential development; (2) commercial developer construction of the 
parking garage (roughly valued at $5-7m for the residential parking) and a financial contribution of $2.9m, and (3) 
affordable housing developer construction of the development with 71 affordable housing units and space for Self Help for 
the Elderly. The mixed-use nature of the project allows the commercial developer to obtain concessions and waivers under 
217 of 570

--- page 218 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
state housing density bonus law for greater floor area and height. 
Typically, partnership projects of this nature require the residential components to be built first, to ensure the commercial 
developer cannot walk away from their commitments on providing housing units. However, given the unique shared 
nature of the parking garage and the project construction phasing, the residential development can only seek final federal 
tax credit financing after parking podium construction, which would create a lag of 1-2 years between the start of the 
commercial superstructure, which benefits from density bonus hinging upon the residential and parking access on City 
land, and the residential superstructure construction.   
  
City staff was concerned that in a worst-case scenario, the commercial project could potentially start operating and 
profiting from the benefits provided from driveway access and shared parking garage on City land (rough collective value 
at $13.5-15m), without meeting its commitment to deliver a housing project. Staff believes the housing component is the 
project lynchpin and without assurances that it would be built to the satisfaction of the City Council, the project cannot 
proceed further.   
To allow for the commercial project to move forward and protect the City’s land interests and contributions, staff 
proposed the following plan to the development team with four stages:  
1.
Certificate of Occupancy (C of O) issuance for the commercial project tied to building permit issuance for 
residential development OR a significant financial contribution (such as $10m) at C of O of the commercial 
project that would enable construction of the affordable housing. If the residential receives tax credit financing 
or is issued building permits prior to C of O, these monies would no longer be required; and  
2.
A secondary deposit of $3m dollars at building permit issuance for the garage, to be used if the residential 
project requires major changes to obtain financing (and to be returned if project meets its schedule of 
performance); and  
3.
Should Alta be unable to proceed, the development team would find an alternate affordable housing partner, 
with City’s approval, who can revise and deliver the affordable housing component within a prescribed 
timeframe; and  
4.
All plans and entitlements to revert to City to find an affordable developer who can deliver the residential 
units or explore alternatives such as a market-rate project.   
The development team has agreed to these terms, with the exception of tying the C of O of the commercial to the 
residential project or holding building permit issuance of commercial building with the residential building, citing financial 
considerations. They are also not willing to provide a $10m dollar true-up at C of O of the commercial building if the 
residential project fails to move forward to account for the contribution of City land towards their project. The 
development team‘s position is that they will have already made a significant monetary investment ($5-$7m) to build the 
parking garage and foundation for the housing structure. Alternatively, they are amenable to a staff proposed minimum 
contribution of $3.5m towards the City’s affordable housing fund at C of O. 
Should the residential project fail despite the above safeguards, the City can utilize the $3m secondary deposit and the 
$3.5-10m true-up contributions at C of O towards other affordable housing projects and could potentially flip the entitled 
property for a market-rate housing development (which would include a subset of inclusionary below market rate 
affordable housing units). Given the long timelines for this project, staff would include adjustments to these assurances to 
account for annual Consumer Price Index increases. 
Should the project has to flip to a market rate project, City staff would likely need to conduct an RFP process to identify a 
market rate developer to build the housing units (vertical portion) of the project. Any potential proceeds received from a 
market rate developer for the long-term lease of City land that includes physical improvements of a parking garage, 
building foundation and project plans could likely be utilized for activities such as affordable housing and City 
218 of 570

--- page 219 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
infrastructure investments. 
Based on the above background and history, staff is seeking direction from City Council on the following scenarios and 
which substantive option to pursue:
       Substantive Options:

Option 1: Extend the ENA to allow staff and development team to continue to negotiate the assurances and 
require greater contributions from the commercial team in the case of failure of a residential project to move 
forward, such as $10m for the access on City land.

Option 2: Develop the DA/DDLA based on the terms described herein with the $3.5m contribution from the 
development team as the final assurance at C of O. 

Option 3: Allow the ENA to expire and explore other potential stand-alone affordable housing projects at this site.  
Project Schedule
Another challenge in the project negotiations has been the Project construction schedule. While the ENA outlined a 10-
year overall timeline, the development team originally proposed 14 years for completion, with a four-year timeline just to 
submit building permit applications. This proposed schedule was not considered a good-faith effort to meet the City’s 
goals of producing housing at this site within the current Housing Element Cycle, which concludes in 2031. In comparison, 
the Kiku Crossing 100% affordable housing project with 225 units and a parking garage with over 525 spaces, was entitled 
and built within four years. 
Staff provided an alternative schedule (Attachment 3) that proposed for construction to be completed by 2031, with some 
buffers built-in to 2035 that account for unforeseen issues, keeping in mind the 10-year timeline in the ENA. This schedule 
was based on a review of other large building permit turnaround times, discussions with the Building Official and Permit 
Center Supervisor and with a written commitment to turn around City reviews expeditiously, with regular check-in 
meetings to resolve issues and ensure progress. Since these discussions, the development team has proposed an alternate 
schedule that would achieve completion of the entire project by December 2032, but with extensions or delays, could be 
delayed as much as four years (end of 2036).  
The biggest difference lies in the applicant submitting the first permit application two years after entitlement and waiting 
two years for permit issuance. This would effectively double the time to see the project break ground for the garage, 
which would significantly delay the affordable housing timeline on seeking tax credit financing (which may take multiple 
rounds, with only one application round per year), as outlined in the table below. The estimated dates below were 
contingent upon approval by October of 2024. Any extensions on the ENA would further delay the completion of the 
project.

The residential units would count towards the current Housing Element cycle if building permits are issued before the end 
of 2031. Based on the above differences, staff is deeply concerned about the project schedule and delivery of units within 
a timely manner and the effects of encumbering City land for the next 12 years without significant assurances that the 
units would get built. Furthermore, given the rise of construction and other development costs over time, the delay on the 
affordable project’s first tax credit application submittal—with no guarantee of success in the initial application round – is 
likely to diminish the value of the City’s $3m contribution and other sources in the Project.   
Given these concerns, staff is seeking direction from City Council on which scheduling option should be included in the 
DA/DDLA:
        Scheduling Options:
•
Option 1: The affordable units should be permitted and constructed in the optimal scenario within this 
Housing Cycle (by 2031). 
•
Option 2: The affordable units should receive construction permits within this Housing Cycle (by 2031).
It should be noted that either of these options will allow the City to get credit for these affordable units during this 
Housing Cycle. However, any delays or extensions in the preceding milestones could affect this timeline. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Depending on Council direction regarding the ENA extension and potential project entitlements, the City would be 
committing its parcel to this project for a 99-year ground lease at $1 per year and providing $3m to support the affordable 
housing development. This project will be subject to all of the City’s development impact fees, including but not limited to 
traffic impact fees, commercial-linkage fees, and park impact fees. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This study session to discuss deal terms is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will 
not cause a physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.) The proposed Bespoke 
development project will be subject to environmental review which is being completed through the entitlement process.
NOTICE PROVIDED:
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

discussions with Self-Help for the Elderly, between our delivery condition and their reserved funds, we
collectively will be able to provide the required capital for them to successfully move into the project.

Outside Sources of Funding

Our development team understands the City’s proposed structure, which consists of the City’s
contribution of the Talbots City Lot, in the form of a long-term ground lease for $1 per year. That said,
the development team prefers a fee simple transfer, but can accommodate the ground lease structure,
if preferred by the City. Additionally, we fully appreciate the importance of minimizing the City’s
financial assistance to the maximum extent possible by applying for multiple outside sources of funding.

In response to this City priority, we have explored, researched and analyzed the outside sources as
detailed in the “Follow-up Proposal Parameters”, and have determined that a Family-oriented project of
our size and scope would qualify for and be able to obtain these outside funding sources.

Project Labor Agreement

The development team shares the City’s priority for supporting local Labor Unions, wherever possible.
The Partnership is open to exploring a potential Project Labor Agreement for this project. In that spirit,
the project team has reached out to the SMC Building Trades Council to initiate that discussion and we
look forward to continuing the dialogue on this topic.

Below please find responses to the specific questions raised in Follow-up Proposal Parameters. If you
have any further questions or comments, our development team would be very happy to answer any
questions you might have. As we have stated in both our original proposal and our previous response to
the City, in the end, our goal is to support the City in achieving its objectives of providing safe, secure
and attractive affordable housing for its residents.

We are confident that if the City were to choose our development team, you would find the Alta
Housing, Prometheus and Harvest Properties team to be one that is collaborative, transparent, and
always willing to have an open dialogue with you, all members of the City’s team, as well as community
stakeholders. Harvest and Prometheus are very proud to be working with Alta Housing on this
exceptional community opportunity. Alta Housing (previously Palo Alto Housing), a community-based
non-profit has been developing and managing affordable housing in San Mateo and Santa Clara Counties
for over 50 years. They are uniquely positioned to manage and provide all the residential supportive
care services that will be needed and required for the residents to thrive.

Working collaboratively with Self Help for the Elderly, in conjunction with the City of San Mateo, we are
committed to working together to create a new development project that serves the multi-generational
needs of the San Mateo community. Thank you again for this opportunity to respond to your questions.
If you should have any further questions or require additional clarification, please do not hesitate to let

us know. Our development team truly looks forward to the next steps in the selection process.

Yours Sincerely,

Preston O’Connell Pat Calihan Carlos Castellanos

1. The TOD Program isn’t reliably available.
1. The previous round in 2020 was the first TOD “NOFA” since 2014.
2. HCD has not announced plans for another TOD NOFA.
3. In addition, TOD funding often lags other programs ($140 million for
TOD in 2020 vs. $160 million for IIG in 2021, $195 million for MHP in
2021, and $297 million for MHP in 2020).
2. HCD guidelines prohibit “subsidy stacking” — one unit cannot benefit from
both MHP and TOD funding (but IIG is exempt).
3. The greater per-unit loan amount available to this project under the MHP
program — described above — is not available under TOD for any project;
therefore, the maximum possible award under TOD will always be less than
the maximum MHP award for this project under current regulations.

7. Would you consider trying to acquire the parcels immediately fronting the Talbot’s parking lot

along B-Street for a larger development site?

The development team is open to acquiring the parcels fronting the Talbot’s parking lot. The
opportunity to transform the full City block will lead to a very exciting development that will
further allow the team to curate a marque project that meets the City’s vision for the
Downtown area. Acquiring the adjacent sites will provide optimal site flexibility, additional
parking stalls, enhanced architectural design, increased programming to activate the public
realm, and provide subsidized rent to offer community serving retail. The team currently
controls the 445 S. B Street (former Talbots Toy Store), which provides many benefits to the City
Lot such as: i) replacement of public parking, ii) on-site parking for the residential units, iii)
Developer Community Benefit Contribution of $2.9M to cover the potential short-fall and
provide a further the ability to provide subterranean parking.

8. Would you consider entering into a Project Labor Agreement for this project?

The development team is open to exploring a Project Labor Agreement for this project and has
reached out to James Ruigomez at the SMC Building Trades Council to discuss the potential to work

together on this project.

1. Ath and Railroad Parking Lot — Bespoke Development Term Sheet

CITY OF SAN MATEO City Hall

www.cityofsanmateo.org

Agenda Report

Agenda Number: 1 Section Name: {{section.name}} Account Number: 10-1311 File ID: {{item.tracking_number}}
TO: City Council

FROM: Drew Corbett, City Manager

PREPARED BY: City Manager's Office

MEETING DATE: May 15, 2023

SUBJECT:

4th and Railroad Parking Lot — Bespoke Development Term Sheet

RECOMMENDATION:
Review and provide feedback on the proposed Term Sheet for the public-private partnership Bespoke development
project, which includes the City-owned parking lot at 4th and Railroad Avenue.

BACKGROUND:

The City is currently party to an Exclusive Negotiating Agreement (ENA) for the redevelopment of the City-owned
surface parking lot located at 4th and Railroad Avenue, directly across the railroad tracks from the Kiku Crossing
development. The ENA is with a three-party development team comprised of Prometheus, Harvest Properties, and
Alta Housing (a non-profit affordable housing developer) and is intended to be in place throughout the entitlement
phase of the project. The City entered into the ENA in October 2021 and has since been working with the
development team to refine the project, now referred to as Bespoke, through the planning entitlement process. The
Bespoke project completed the pre-application phase of its planning review and submitted their formal planning
application in February 2023.

The Bespoke project will redevelop the entire block located between B Street and Railroad Avenue between 4th and
5th Avenues. The project includes 60-units of affordable housing, 150,000 square feet of office space, 12,000 square
feet of retail space, and approximately 4,500 square feet of community space intended for Self-Help for the Elderly. A
shared underground parking garage will be built across the site which will include 38 residential parking spaces and 80
commercial spaces.

As the project has evolved, so have the deal terms related to the City’s provision of land and funding support for the
affordable housing component of the project. This report is intended to provide the City Council with an update on
the project status and to get Council feedback on the proposed deal terms as outlined in the Term Sheet, included as
Attachment 1. In addition to the Term Sheet, the development team has provided a letter outlining their commitment
to the project, included as Attachment 2.

Developer Selection History

The City Council adopted a resolution declaring the surface parking lot at East 4th Avenue and South Railroad Avenue,
known as the Talbot’s parking lot, as “Surplus Land” at the January 4, 2021 City Council meeting. Following this
declaration, staff released a Notice of Surplus Lands Availability to all housing developers that have registered with the
State Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD). In response to this notice, the City received four
letters of interest for the development of affordable housing on the site.

The City requested that the interested parties respond with a proposal responding to the following criteria:

CITY OF SAN MATEO

RE: Bespoke Cover Letter
April 28", 2023

City Council of San Mateo
City of San Mateo

330 W 20" Ave

San Mateo, CA 94403

Dear Council Members,

Over the past two and a half years, our collective team has engaged with City Staff, the Planning
Commission, and the previous and current City Council on our public private partnership project, Bespoke,
encompassing the entire city block bounded by 4*" and 5" Avenue, B Street, and Railroad Avenue. We are
excited to come back in front of you this calendar year to reaffirm our previously stated goal of providing
affordable housing, developing an extension to the successful B Street Retail environment, being a
champion for local businesses, and designing a project that maintains the fabric of the surrounding
neighborhood.

The purpose of this letter is to provide a brief overview of the applicant team, the history of the Bespoke
project, what we have heard from the community during the course of our development, and a summary
of the current development project.

Development Team

The applicant team is comprised of Harvest Properties, Prometheus Real Estate Group, and Alta Housing
- all locally-based, highly experienced, and community-focused developers. Harvest and Prometheus have
been active in the San Mateo market for over five decades and currently manage approximately 320
residential units and 850,000 square feet of office space in San Mateo. Prometheus is currently developing
the former Trag’s site located at 303 Baldwin site in Downtown San Mateo, which will create a high-
quality, uniquely designed building with the goal of enhancing the public realm near the Downtown
Caltrain station and serve as the company’s new headquarters. Harvest properties is currently under
construction on a 290 unit home development that will replace four office building totaling 225,000
square feet and provide a significant amount of for-sale entry level housing. Alta Housing has been
committed to providing affordable housing in Silicon Valley since its inception over 50 years ago with the
help of local community leaders. Known for innovative development, outstanding resident support, and
high-quality maintenance of its buildings, Alta Housing manages over 850 units to house more than 2,000
low-income residents across the Bay Area.

History of Bespoke
The history of the Bespoke project dates back to the summer of 2020 when the applicant team started to
have informal discussions with the City of San Mateo regarding its parcel on 4° Avenue and Railroad

Page 1 of 3

275 of 570


--- page 276 ---
ALTA
ALTA. = HARVEST © PROMETHEUS

Avenue. Those discussions subsequently developed into a Request for Proposals from the City of San
Mateo which formally accepted the applicant’s proposal in the second quarter of 2021. Since then, the
applicant team has collaborated with City Staff to execute an Exclusive Negotiating Agreement and meet
an agreed upon schedule to submit the Preliminary Title Report, Phase | and Geotechnical Studies, Pre-
Application, and Formal Entitlements Application in addition to completing the applicant-led CEQA studies
and commencing the CEQA scoping necessary for the project. Throughout the past two and a half years,
the applicant team has collaborated with City Staff and spent millions of dollars in pursuit of the best
development project.

What Our Team Has Heard

The development team has spent considerable time reaching out to and connecting with San Mateo
residents, local retail partners, and City Leadership throughout official City-led sessions to informal coffee
meetings in Downtown. The themes we have heard from these various sessions are as follows:

Provide affordable housing

Champion local businesses

Create an active retail environment
Concentrate jobs around transit
Maintain the fabric of the neighborhoods

ro,

Our development project has responded to these themes and we are excited to share with the City Council
our currently proposed project.

Bespoke Project Summary

The Bespoke project includes 60 residential units, an increase of eight units from the Request for Proposal
response, that will be designated as family-friendly units, meaning that over 50% of the units are two- and
three- bedroom — housing up to 260 individuals. All of the units will be income restricted between the
30% to 80% area medium income threshold. The housing project is supported by 38 dedicated parking
spaces in addition to spaces shared with the commercial office after typical working hours. The ground
floor of the housing project includes a dedicated space for Self Help for the Elderly, a local non-profit
stalwart of San Mateo, which provides services to over 40,000 seniors in the San Mateo community and
the Bay Area.

The curated development locates approximately 12,000 square feet of retail storefront space along B
Street, wrapping around the corner onto 4* and 5‘ Avenues, allowing for the extension of San Mateo’s
already lively downtown core. Control over the entire block allows for the project to put forth a continuous
design aesthetic that improves the pedestrian experience across the entire block and reflects the City’s
stated goals regarding pedestrians in the downtown.

The commercial portion of the project includes a total of ~150,000 square feet of office space. This office

project reflects the City’s stated desire of developing new commercial office in smart locations; i.e. close

Page 2 of 3

276 of 570


--- page 277 ---
ALTA
HOUSING [WEB M Ee! © PROMETHEUS

to transit and close to existing retail cores — helping to provide a stable daytime population in support of
downtown. This project ticks both of those boxes. Secondly, the project strives to be on the leading edge
of sustainable design including a mass timber structure, LEED Gold Certification as well as WELL Building
Core & Shell Certification, and Carbon Neutrality. Finally, the commercial project will pay approximately
$20 million in impact fees and $4.3 million in commercial linkage fees.

To allow this collective project to move forward, the following commitments by the City and the
Commercial developer are included:

x

* City contribution of $2.75M to the housing project in the form of a residual receipts loan

* City to contribute the city-owned parking lot via a non-escalating 99-year ground lease at $1 per
year

* Commercial Developer to contribute $2.9M to the affordable housing project

* Commercial Developer to finance and construct the parking garage inclusive of the housing

project’s parking

xX

<

<

Greater detail on all of the project’s deal terms can be found in the Term Sheet document.

We appreciate the time to update the City Council as to the status of our public-private partnership and
hope that Bespoke can serve as a template for future public-private partnerships where a commercial
development can provide economic support to catalyze housing production for the community. We look

forward to discussing the project’s deal terms in greater detail with you all.

Sincerely,
The collective Alta, Harvest, and Prometheus Team

RE: Carpenters Union Local 217 Support for 445 South B St.
Dear: Mayor Lee & Fellow Councilmembers,

The members of Carpenters Local 217 in San Mateo and the surrounding Bay Area
strongly support the approval and development of 445 South B Ave. This project will not
only provide much-needed housing in the area, it will also generate hundreds of union
construction jobs in San Mateo and provide an opportunity for local apprentices,
including women, minorities, veterans and at-risk youth, to begin or continue a career in
the construction industry.

The Bespoke project has highlighted the best in what development should look like in
the City of San Mateo. With the creation of 60 housing units, and 160 thousand sf. of
commercial space, this development falls in line with the goals of both the City and
County when it comes to adding affordable housing, walkable ecosystems and creating
viable work opportunities for the construction workers in the area. While creating an
economic opportunity that will help create small business diversification within the City,
promoting equity and inclusion and adding additional tax revenue to the City.

The Bespoke project will enhance the City of San Mateo for many years to come and by
creating opportunities for local residents and businesses, San Mateo will continue to lead
the way on what responsible development looks like.

With this Development Team's commitment to the City of San Mateo, the Carpenters Union,
residents, and our community at large, we urge you to support this project.

Sincerely,

Ed Evans

Senior Field Representative

www.selfhelpelderty.org Providing strength, hope and empowerment to seniors since 1966

Dear City Staff, Planning Commissioners,
and City Council Members,

As a resident of San Mateo and a friend of Self-Help for the Elderly Senior Activity Center in
San Mateo Central Park, I am wniting to express my support for the mixed-use development
project, Bespoke (lead developer is Harvest Properties), which will offer Self-Help a permanent
home for its Senior Activity Center.

For over 30 years, Self-Help for the Elderly (SHE) has been dedicated to serving seniors in the
City of San Mateo regardless of race, culture, gender or physical capability. In year of 2020-
2021, the organization served over 30,000 meals. At the height of the pandemic, SHE quickly
pivoted and was able to convert dine-in meal service to take-out and delivery orders. It also
expanded its home delivery service to reach the North Central district of San Mateo with an
additional 15,000 home delivery meals, demonstrating SHE’s unwavering commitment to
meeting the critical needs of our cherished community members.

However, as you are no doubt aware, the organization's current Central Park facility lease will
expire June 2025 in anticipation of the launch of the Central Park improvement project. The
Bespoke project offers a timely and much-needed solution to this challenge, providing a new
permanent home for Self-Help for the Elderly that is thoughtfully designed with the
organization's program and functional needs in mind. The proposed space consists of
approximately 4,500 sq. ft., and it will be delivered at substantially below market rent.

Valuing family is a core value of the SHE Senior Activity Center and its seniors. In addition to
providing SHE a permanent home, the Bespoke project plans 60 units of much needed family-
friendly 100% affordable housing, bringing multi-generational workforce housing to Downtown
San Mateo and helping to concentrate jobs around transit.

As a member of the community of San Mateo, I strongly support the timely approval of the
Bespoke project so that the future home for Self-Help for the Elderly can be delivered as soon as
possible. This project will have a profound and positive impact on our community by creating
jobs, providing much-needed affordable housing, and last but not least, providing the home
urgently needed by Self-Help for the Elderly, an essential local non-profit organization.

Thank you for your careful consideration of this letter, and for all that you do to make our
community the vibrant and thriving place that I call “home”.

Sincerely,

(See cdtec hok )

San Francisco San Mateo ’ South Bay , Alameda & Contra Costa

CITY OF SAN MATEO

307 of 570

--- page 308 ---
Dear San Mateo City Council,
The proposed development will bring 71 family-friendly affordable housing units to San Mateo,
addressing a critical shortage of affordable housing in the city. This project represents a vital step
forward in ensuring that working families, and others in need have access to stable and affordable
homes.
The Harvest Development team has demonstrated an impressive commitment to ensuring the
success of this project. The revised deal terms bring the total equity contribution to $9.4 million,
including an additional $6.5 million as a guarantee in the unlikely event that financing for the
affordable housing component is not obtained or is significantly delayed. This level of financial
commitment goes well beyond what is typically expected of a commercial developer, and we believe
it reflects the importance of the project to the community.
Additionally, the Commercial team’s commitment in building the subterranean garage shared
between residential and commercial spaces, along with the payment of the commercial linkage fee
to support future affordable housing projects in the City of San Mateo, demonstrates their dedication
to comprehensive community-building and housing production.
I strongly urge the City Council to approve these updated terms so that the project can move forward
through the entitlement and development process without further delay.
Timothy Clark
308 of 570

--- page 309 ---
Building an equitable and sustainable future, together.

City of San Mateo

City Council

330 West 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403

Monday, September 16th, 2024

Re: Support for Bespoke Development in Downtown San Mateo

Dear Mayor Nash, Deputy Mayor Newsom, Council Member Loraine, Council Member
Hedges, and Council Member Lee:

As a member of Peninsula for Everyone, a non-profit organization that advocates for
abundant, affordable housing and inclusive, sustainable communities for all, | am writing to
express our strong support for the Bespoke project in Downtown San Mateo. This project is
of great importance due to the much-needed affordable housing it will provide to the
community.

The proposed development will bring 71 family-friendly affordable housing units to San
Mateo, addressing a critical shortage of affordable housing in the city. This project
represents a vital step forward in ensuring that working families, and others in need have
access to stable and affordable homes.

The Harvest Development team has demonstrated an impressive commitment to ensuring
the success of this project. The revised deal terms bring the total equity contribution to
$9.4 million, including an additional $6.5 million as a guarantee in the unlikely event that
financing for the affordable housing component is not obtained or is significantly delayed.
This level of financial commitment goes well beyond what is typically expected of a
commercial developer, and we believe it reflects the importance of the project to the
community.

Additionally, the Commercial team's commitment in building the subterranean garage
shared between residential and commercial spaces, along with the payment of the

309 of 570


--- page 310 ---
commercial linkage fee to support future affordable housing projects in the City of San
Mateo, demonstrates their dedication to comprehensive community-building and housing
production.

We strongly urge the City Council to approve these updated terms so that the project can
move forward through the entitlement and development process without further delay.
The proposed completion timeline of 2032 is both efficient and realistic, as it allows
sufficient time for the design and coordination necessary to deliver a high-quality and
complex mixed-use project that will be a long-term asset to the community and meet the
housing needs of San Mateo's residents.

Should the project be denied or further delayed, it would result in at least a two-year
setback to restart the entitlement process without the commercial component. It would
jeopardize not only the delivery of 71 units of affordable housing within the current
housing cycle but also the broader community benefits this project provides. It is crucial
that the City Council allows this project to proceed, ensuring affordable housing for those
who need it most.

We respectfully request the City Council's approval of the updated deal terms for this
important development. Thank you for your time and thoughtful consideration.

Leora Tanjuakco Ross

Leora Tanjuatco Ross
Peninsula for Everyone

Building an equitable and sustainable future, together.

310 of 570


--- page 311 ---
From:
David Hunt
To:
City Council (San Mateo)
Cc:
Clerk
Subject:
Bespoke Development - Share Parking with Kiku
Date:
Monday, September 16, 2024 7:35:16 AM
Hello Council,
I’m writing in regard to the Bespoke development topic on the agenda. Council should
recommend that staff work with the developer to share parking with the grossly underutilized
Kiku Crossing garage in order to increase the housing units that can be built in this
development. Selecting this development layout based on “offering the highest ratio of onsite
parking for the housing” is ignoring the climate crisis we’re in and not taking appropriate
measures to utilize a large, already built garage. Reducing VMT is critical to addressing climate
change and one of the biggest levers we have to pull is housing near existing high-quality
transit. 
If council insists on prioritizing parking over housing then they should work with the developer
to unbundle parking from the rent so that residents that don’t have cars aren’t burdened with
the cost of this unneeded residential garage. Parking related costs will likely be 15-20% of the
rent cost in this building. Please give the option for residents that can’t afford a car to be able to
afford housing. 
As a city there are very limited properties within convenient walking distance to Caltrain.
Please work with the developer to come up with a plan to utilize the ample parking at Kiku
Crossing and add more units to this development.
David Hunt

From:
Michael
To:
City Council (San Mateo)
Cc:
Martin McTaggart
Subject:
9/16/24 City Council Special Meeting Item #2 Bespoke
Date:
Monday, September 16, 2024 9:03:56 AM
Greeting Mayor Nash and Councilmembers - I'm writing to voice my strong disapproval of the
proposal for Bespoke to develop the commercial portion of this project first with assurances to
build housing later. We do not need more commercial - we need affordable housing and we
need it now. Developers have proven time and again they cannot be trusted - we have dirt lots
and concrete holes sitting idle for years that prove it. No dollar amount of so-called assurances
will make this guaranteed - do not fall for it. If Alta, Harvest and Prometheus cannot deliver
the entire project, then the city should terminate its exclusive agreement with these developers
and find one that will deliver the entire project. No more gifts to developers - do not yield on
this - the city will rightly be viewed as a bunch of gullible suckers if we fall for this ploy.
Thank you for listening - Michael Weinhauer

From:
David Light
To:
City Council (San Mateo)
Subject:
Item #2 “Bespoke” - Special City Council Meeting Today
Date:
Monday, September 16, 2024 1:30:28 PM
Dear Mayor Diaz Nash and City Council Members,
I have alway heard that repeating the same thing over and over again while expecting a
different result is not good. We need affordable housing and not more empty office space.
Developers need to build the housing before or at the same time as new office space and not as
an after thought.
San Mateo has a lot of empty new “boutique” office space and empty, unleased traditional
office space in beautiful old building that can be refurbished. What San Mateo does not have a
lot of is affordable housing. Please, address our major problem and not be guided by the goals
of developers.
In a recent Planning Commission meeting, I was moved to see one member vote “No” on a
project due to the fact that it did not address housing needs near to public transportation. San
Mateo needs more leaders to focus on achieving our stated goals.
Thank you!
-David Light

in ensuring that working families and others in need have access to stable, affordable homes,

16 SEPTEMBER 2024
San Mateo City Council
330 West 20th Avenue
San Mateo City CA 94403
RE: 4th and Railroad Parking Lot - Bespoke Development
Dear Honorable Mayor & City Council Members;
On behalf of the Building & Construction Trades Council of San Mateo
County (representing over 10,000 local working families), we are excited for
the proposed Bespoke development.
While we consider development utilizing state prevailing wage standards is a
necessary beneﬁt it is only one step in the right direction
The building trade afﬁliates request the inclusion of community work force
beneﬁts that will ensure residents who support their families by working in
the construction trades are recognized for their value as community
members, more career pathways are encouraged through the use of joint
labor management apprenticeship training programs, employer/contractors
awarded project work at every tier (primary contractor, & subcontractors)
are law abiding employers and they can be held accountable for wages, and
working conditions of their employed workers.
Furthermore, we request that the prevailing wage requirement not be lost in
transition due to the affordable housing development optional transfer to
market rate housing if development does not proceed as planned.
I look forward to discussing these proposed changes. It is the goal of the
building trades council to promote and sustain a vibrant local economy and
invest in the working class of San Mateo City.
Sincerely
Bart M. Pantoja
Business Manager | Financial Secretary-Treasurer

Members of the San Mateo City Council,
This proposed development will bring 71 family-friendly affordable housing units to San Mateo,
addressing the critical shortage of affordable housing in the city. This project represents a vital step
forward in ensuring that working families have access to stable and affordable homes.
The Harvest Development team has demonstrated an impressive commitment to ensuring the
success of this project. The revised deal terms bring the total equity contribution to $9.4 million,
including an additional $6.5 million as a guarantee in the unlikely event that financing for the
affordable housing component is not obtained or is significantly delayed. This level of financial
commitment goes well beyond what is typically expected of a commercial developer, and it reflects
the importance of the project to the community.
Additionally, the Commercial team’s commitment in building the subterranean garage shared
between residential and commercial spaces, along with the payment of the commercial linkage fee
to support future affordable housing projects in the City of San Mateo, demonstrates their dedication
to comprehensive community-building and housing production.
I was a resident of San Mateo for several years, as a renter. I might still live in San Mateo, if the city
were not suffering from a severe housing shortage. When we decided to become homeowners, we
did look at many homes in San Mateo -- we looked at more than a hundred properties around the
County, over the course of about six months -- we found ourselves completely priced out of the city.
San Mateo County has added more than ten jobs, for each new home, year after year, for more than
a decade, and the City of San Mateo has contributed to this deficit. The City must take aggressive
action in smoothing the path for more homes of all types to be built. You should not be throwing up
new road blocks or imposing more costs -- costs that ultimately are borne by the people who should
be living in these homes.
I strongly urge the City Council to approve these updated terms so that the project can move forward
through the entitlement and development process without further delay.
Auros Harman

September 16, 2024
San Mateo City Council
San Mateo City Hall
330 West 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403
Re: Support Bespoke - Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County
Dear Members of the San Mateo City Council,
The Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County (HLC) works with our
communities and their leaders to produce and preserve quality affordable homes.
On behalf of HLC, I’m writing to express our support for the 71 new affordable homes in
the Bespoke proposal by Alta Housing. With 44 of these slated to be 2- and 3-bedroom
homes, the proposal has the potential to provide much-needed stability to 44 of your
lower-income families, allowing them to continue supporting your community—whether
through their patronage or employment by San Mateo’s small businesses. These are
the community members who make your city such an attractive place to call home, yet
they struggle to find, afford, and maintain suitable housing.
The solutions aren’t easy, but they exist—and the Bespoke proposal is one of them. We
urge you to approve a deal term that gives Alta Housing the best chance for success. In
doing so, you will ensure that more low-income families in your community can thrive.
Sincerely,
Ken Chan
Senior Organizer
Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County

Contract Amendment
Page 1 of 2
AMENDMENT NO. 2 TO THE EXCLUSIVE NEGOTIATING RIGHTS AGREEMENT 
DOWNTOWN SAN MATEO AFFORDABLE HOUSING SITE
This Amendment No. 2 is made and entered into this day ___of March, 2025, by and between 
the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
("CITY"), and DTSM Housing LLC, a Delaware limited liability Company ("DEVELOPER").
              
WHEREAS, City and Developer entered into an EXCLUSIVE NEGOTIATING RIGHTS AGREEMENT 
(“ENA”) for the development of a mixed use project located in downtown San Mateo.
              WHEREAS, City and Developer amended that certain ENA dated October 14, 2021, through 
Amendment No. 1 on October 9, 2024, to extend the term of the Negotiating Period to April 14, 2025, 
and further wish to amend the ENA to extend the term to October 14, 2025.
WHEREAS, the City Council considered this Amendment No. 2 on March 17, 2025 and 
authorized the City Manager to execute this Amendment No. 2. 
NOW, THEREFORE, the parties agree as follows:
1.
The Negotiating Period and associated timelines shall be extended by six months until 
October 14, 2025.
2.
The remaining terms of the ENA that do not conflict with this Amendment No. 2 shall 
remain in full force and effect.

SUBJECT:
2024 Annual Progress Reports: Housing Element, General Plan, and Housing Successor Agency - Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve the Annual Progress Reports and direct the City Manager to submit the 2024 General Plan Annual Progress 
Report to the California Office of Policy and Research; the 2024 Housing Element Annual Progress Report to the California 
Department of Housing and Community Development; and the 2024 Housing Successor Agency Annual Report to the 
California Department of Finance. 
BACKGROUND:
Government Code Sections 65400 and 65700 require local planning agencies to submit an Annual Progress Report (APR) 
on the status of their General Plan and Housing Element implementation to the local legislative body. The APR is then 
submitted to California Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) and Office of Planning and Research 
(OPR) by April 1st of each year. 
California Health and Safety Code Section 34176.1(f) similarly requires jurisdictions to submit an annual Housing Successor 
Agency Report by the same deadline to the California Department of Finance (DoF). This report provides details of the 
housing funds, assets, and activities of the City of San Mateo, which is acting as the Housing Successor Agency of the 
former Redevelopment Agency of the City of San Mateo.
This agenda report contains the General Plan and Housing Element Annual Progress Reports (Attachments 1 and 2) and 
Housing Successor Report (Attachment 3) for review and approval by City Council. An overview of each of the three state-
mandated reports is provided below.
Housing Element Annual Progress Report Summary
While the Housing Element is a part of the General Plan, the Housing Element APR is subject to additional and specific
requirements not applicable to other elements of the General Plan. The Housing Element APR has been prepared pursuant 
to California Government Code Section 65400 and includes a detailed accounting of all housing projects submitted, 
reviewed, entitled, and constructed in the City in 2024. The APR also provides an update on the implementation on each of 
the Housing Element’s policies and programs to achieve the City’s housing vision. 
 
This is the second reporting year of the City’s 2023-2031 Housing Element for the sixth housing cycle, which was adopted 
by the City Council May 20, 2024 and determined to be in substantial compliance with Housing Element law by HCD on July 
23, 2024. It includes the following information:

Progress report on implementation of the Housing Element programs (Attachment 1 - Table D)


Progress in meeting the City’s Regional Housing Needs Allocation (RHNA) by income level (Tables A2 and B)

Information on the types of housing units that were issued building permits, as well as those that were completed 
(Table A2) 

Information on number of planning applications submitted, as well as those that were approved (Table A) 

Information on the City’s local preference policy (Table K)


o

Progress Towards RHNA Targets
The City continued to make meaningful progress towards its RHNA in 2024, including the following achievements:

303 Baldwin Ave., a mixed-use project with 64 rental units, including 6 very low-income BMR units, received its 
certificate of occupancy; 

Kiku Crossing, a 225-unit affordable housing development on City-owned land, received its final certificate of 
occupancy on August 16, 2024 and reached full occupancy at the end of 2024;

Peninsula Heights, a 290-unit multifamily for-sale development on a former office campus site that includes 29 
low-income BMR units, received its building permits; 

477 9th Ave., a mixed-use project on a former office site that includes 120 rental units, including 12 very low-
income units, was entitled (building permit issued March 2025);  

91 Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) applications received and 66 ADU permits issued; and

Two SB 9 projects processed.

completed/constructed (certificate of occupancy). Per HCD definition, applications submitted are applications that have 
been determined complete by the jurisdiction. Please note that building permit is the milestone HCD uses to count units 
towards a jurisdiction’s RHNA progress.
